source: USDA Cooperative Extension publications + Washington State University Extension + Cornell Cooperative Extension + The Old Farmer's Almanac. Conservative ranges suitable for general North American gardening; coastal, mountain, and desert microclimates may shift dates 2-3 weeks.

windows.notes: 6-8 weeks before last frost; 70-75°F germination
Harden off 7-10 days; soil should reach 60°F

entries.notes: Pinch suckers on indeterminate varieties. Stake or cage. Mulch heavily to maintain even moisture and reduce blossom-end rot.

maritime.notes: Choose short-season determinates (55-70d). Cool slow-warming soil — wait until soil hits 60°F before transplant. Late-blight pressure high; stake/prune for airflow.

mediterranean.notes: Deep weekly watering beats shallow daily; avoid overhead irrigation to limit foliar disease. Long warm season favors indeterminates.

continental.notes: Wall-of-Water or row cover lets you transplant 1-2 weeks earlier. Short window favors determinates with predictable harvest.

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat builds fast — get plants in early. Choose VFN/TSWV-resistant varieties; mulch heavily; consider a second fall crop started in midsummer.

arid.notes: Big seedlings struggle in extreme heat — start later for compact transplants. 30% shade cloth above 95°F preserves fruit set; mulch and drip-irrigate.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure stresses transplants — provide windbreak and stake early. Mulch heavily to retain irrigation moisture.

windows.notes: 8-10 weeks before last frost; bottom heat helps
Wait until nights are reliably above 55°F

entries.notes: Slow to germinate (10-14 days at 75-85°F). Black plastic mulch increases soil temp.

maritime.notes: Maritime nights often stay below the 60°F minimum peppers need for fruit set — choose cool-tolerant varieties (Lipstick, Carmen) and use row cover or black plastic to lift soil temps.

mediterranean.notes: Long warm season is ideal; choose full-season varieties for best yield. Deep mulch retains moisture through summer drought.

continental.notes: Short window — pick early-maturing varieties. Pre-warm soil with black plastic 2 weeks before transplant.

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat and humidity favor bacterial spot — choose resistant varieties. Mulch keeps roots cool. Possible fall crop in zones 8b+.

arid.notes: Transplant earlier to set fruit before extreme heat shuts blossoms. 30% shade cloth above 95°F prevents sunscald and blossom drop.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection critical — peppers are brittle; cage or stake. Drip irrigation preferred over overhead.

windows.notes: 8-10 weeks before last frost

entries.notes: Habaneros and superhots benefit from 12+ weeks indoors. Lower water as fruit ripens for hotter flavor.

maritime.notes: Cool maritime nights below 60°F stall fruit set — choose short-season hot peppers (jalapeño, hungarian wax) over slow-ripening superhots (ghost, reaper, scorpion). Black plastic mulch + row cover lifts soil temps.

mediterranean.notes: Long warm season is ideal for full ripening of superhots. Deep mulch retains moisture; capsaicin actually intensifies under mild drought stress.

continental.notes: Pre-warm soil with black plastic; pick early-maturing hot peppers (jalapeño, anaheim, hungarian wax) over slow superhots that won't finish before frost.

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat builds fast — get plants in early. Bacterial spot pressure intense; choose resistant varieties; mulch keeps roots cool.

arid.notes: Transplant earlier to set fruit before extreme heat. 30% shade cloth above 100°F prevents sunscald; capsaicin intensifies in heat-stressed plants.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection critical — peppers are brittle; cage or stake. Drip irrigation preferred over overhead.

windows.notes: Needs warm soil — wait 3 weeks past last frost in cool zones

entries.notes: Sensitive to cold; black plastic mulch and row cover early help.

maritime.notes: Most heat-loving common solanaceous crop — truly marginal in maritime. Choose Asian/Italian thin-skinned cultivars (Ping Tung Long, Rosa Bianca) which set fruit at lower temps; black plastic mulch + low tunnel essential.

mediterranean.notes: Excellent — long warm season ideal. Deep weekly watering; mulch heavily.

continental.notes: Pre-warm soil weeks ahead with black plastic; choose early varieties (Patio Baby, Hansel, Fairy Tale) over full-season Italians.

humid_subtropical.notes: Flea beetle pressure heavy in spring — row cover until flowering. Long warm season allows double cropping in zones 9+.

arid.notes: Heat-tolerant, but afternoon shade above 105°F prevents sunscald. Mulch heavily; deep weekly watering.

semi_arid.notes: Wind shreds large leaves — stake or cage. Consistent drip irrigation; mulch heavily.

windows.notes: Plant seed potatoes when soil is workable; cut pieces with 2 eyes each

entries.notes: Hill soil up around stems as they grow. Use certified seed potatoes to avoid disease.
Self-incompatible — plant at least 2 for pollination.

maritime.notes: Slightly more heat-tolerant than tomato but still cool-night sensitive. Plant 2+ for cross-pollination — single plants are unproductive.

mediterranean.notes: Long warm season ideal; 2+ plants needed for pollination.

continental.notes: Short-season; plant 2 for pollination; harvest when papery husks split.

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat-loving — productive through long summer. Sprawling habit benefits from caging.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; afternoon shade above 100°F preserves fruit set; deep weekly watering.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on lanky stems — cage or stake early.

entries.notes: Harvest fallen fruit; husks turn papery. Self-sows freely.

maritime.notes: Drops fruit when ripe — mulch with straw to keep clean and easy to gather.

mediterranean.notes: Long productive season; deep mulch retains moisture.

continental.notes: Heat-loving but tolerant of short seasons; productive even in zone 4.

humid_subtropical.notes: Long warm season; self-seeds prolifically — manage volunteers each spring.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; deep mulch retains scarce moisture.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on sprawling plants — windbreak helps young plants establish.

windows.notes: Soak seeds 12 hours before planting
Optional in short-season zones; transplant carefully — sensitive roots

entries.notes: Pick pods at 2-4 inches; older pods get tough. Wear gloves — spines irritate skin.

maritime.notes: Truly marginal — soil rarely reaches 70°F that okra needs. Black plastic mulch + low tunnel may produce a thin crop in zone 8b warm summers; consider skipping.

mediterranean.notes: Productive once summer heat sets in. Deep mulch through dry season.

continental.notes: Soak seed 24h before sowing; black plastic mulch essential. Pick small (3 in pods) — even a short crop is rewarding.

humid_subtropical.notes: Signature regional crop — long productive season. Pick daily once producing; gloves help with fine spines.

arid.notes: Heat-loving — soil warms fast. Irrigation mandatory; mulch heavily.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind exposure on tall stalks — stake mid-summer.

windows.notes: 6-8 weeks before last frost for spring crop
Tolerates light frost
Fall crop: sow 12-8 weeks before first fall frost

entries.notes: Bolts in heat — use heat-tolerant varieties for late spring. After main head, lateral shoots produce for weeks.

maritime.notes: Maritime climate is excellent — cool springs and falls extend the harvest. Watch for cabbage moth — row cover is the cleanest defense.

mediterranean.notes: Best as a fall-through-spring crop. Skip summer entirely.

continental.notes: Short spring window — transplants only. Heavy fall production in zones 5-7.

humid_subtropical.notes: Skip spring — heat arrives too fast. Fall sowing late August through October is the productive window. Choose heat-tolerant varieties (Belstar).

arid.notes: Spring window very short — transplant as early as soil works. Shade cloth in late spring extends harvest.

semi_arid.notes: Hardy in fall; spring depends on moisture. Wind protection helps small plants establish.

entries.notes: Blanch white varieties by tying outer leaves over the head. Self-blanching and colored cultivars skip this step.

maritime.notes: Maritime climate is excellent — cool springs and falls match cauliflower's narrow temperature tolerance. Watch for cabbage moth.

mediterranean.notes: Strict fall-winter-spring; skip summer. Self-blanching cultivars (Snowball, Self Blanche).

continental.notes: Short spring window — transplants only. Reliable fall crop in zones 5-7.

humid_subtropical.notes: Primarily fall — spring window too short. Choose heat-tolerant Snow Crown.

arid.notes: Spring window short — transplant as early as soil works. Shade cloth in late spring extends harvest.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on developing curds; consistent moisture critical.

entries.notes: Heads split when over-watered after maturity. Cut and lift on a cool morning.

maritime.notes: Excellent — long cool season. Storage cultivars (Brunswick, Late Flat Dutch) for fall harvest; cabbage moth row cover.

mediterranean.notes: Fall-winter-spring; storage cabbages mature in late fall.

continental.notes: Both spring and fall crops productive. Cold-hardy cultivars (January King, Brunswick) for fall storage.

humid_subtropical.notes: Primarily fall — spring window too short for full heads. Heat-tolerant cultivars (Bravo, Bronco).

arid.notes: Spring window short; choose early varieties (Early Jersey Wakefield); shade cloth extends harvest.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on developing heads; consistent drip irrigation.

windows.notes: Long season — start in late spring for fall harvest

entries.notes: Flavor improves after light frost. Top plants 4 weeks before harvest to encourage uniform sprout sizing.

maritime.notes: Signature crop — cool wet falls produce sweetest sprouts after frost. 100+ day varieties (Diablo, Long Island Improved, Jade Cross).

mediterranean.notes: Fall-winter only — coastal central California works; not for southern CA.

continental.notes: Start in summer for fall harvest; cold sweetens flavor — wait for first frost before harvesting.

humid_subtropical.notes: Marginal — needs cool autumns. Only zone 7 and colder produce reliably; choose Long Island Improved.

arid.notes: Too hot — skip unless under 50% shade in fall; high desert (zone 6 mountains) works.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall stalks — stake; consistent moisture critical for sprout fill.

windows.notes: Cold-hardy; tolerates 20°F
Sweetens after frost

entries.notes: Pick outer leaves first; plant keeps producing. Overwinters in zones 7+ under row cover.

maritime.notes: Kale's signature region — overwinters easily, sweetest after fall frosts. Aphids and cabbage moths are the main pests; row cover helps.

mediterranean.notes: Best as a fall-through-spring crop; summer kale gets bitter and aphid-prone.

continental.notes: Hardy to ~10°F; mulch heavily for winter harvest in zones 6+.

humid_subtropical.notes: Sow later in fall than the calendar suggests — kale can't tolerate the long humid summer. Best window is Sept-Nov for winter harvest.

arid.notes: Spring window very short before heat. Fall sowing under shade cloth, removed as temps drop, is the most productive approach.

semi_arid.notes: Hardy and drought-tolerant once established; deep weekly watering works well.

entries.notes: Most cold-hardy of the cabbage family. Sweetens after frost.
Harvest at 2-3 inches; larger bulbs get woody. Eat raw or cooked.

maritime.notes: Excellent cool-summer crop; succession sow every 3 weeks for continuous bulbs.

mediterranean.notes: Spring and fall — quick 60-day crop.

continental.notes: Fast and forgiving; multiple successions per season.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window — quick harvest before heat. Main crop in fall.

arid.notes: Sow early spring; skip summer; resume in fall.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on bulb development; consistent moisture.

entries.notes: Bolts in heat; spring crop must finish before warm weather. Ideal for fall.

maritime.notes: Excellent cool-summer crop; succession sow every 3 weeks. Flea beetle pressure under row cover.

mediterranean.notes: Spring + fall; bolts fast in heat — skip summer.

continental.notes: Fast crop; succession sow until temps rise; resume in late summer.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window — primarily a fall crop. Choose bolt-resistant varieties (Joi Choi).

arid.notes: Sow early; skip summer; resume in fall under 30% shade cloth.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection; flea beetle pressure under row cover.

windows.notes: Best as a fall crop — bolts if started too early in spring

entries.notes: Use for kimchi. Harvest before hard freeze.
Harvest young leaves for salads, mature for cooking. Bolts in heat.

windows.notes: Fall crop sweeter after frost

entries.notes: Greens are edible too. Harvest roots at 2-3 inches for tender flavor.

maritime.notes: Excellent — cool falls produce sweetest roots. Salad turnips (Hakurei, Tokyo Cross) ready in 35 days.

mediterranean.notes: Fall-winter; skip summer — bolts in heat.

continental.notes: Both spring and fall crops; cold-storage friendly (Purple Top White Globe).

humid_subtropical.notes: Primarily fall — spring window short.

arid.notes: Sow early; skip summer; resume fall under shade cloth initially.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on greens; consistent moisture.

entries.notes: Long season; flavor improves after frost. Store cool for winter use.

windows.notes: Optional; transplant before roots circle the pot

entries.notes: Trellising saves space and reduces disease. Pick frequently to keep plants producing.

maritime.notes: Cool soil delays germination; start indoors and pre-warm beds with black plastic. Powdery mildew pressure high — pick resistant varieties (Marketmore 76, Diva).

mediterranean.notes: Trellis to save space and reduce disease. Deep mulch retains moisture through dry summer.

continental.notes: Short warm window — pre-warm soil and pick early varieties (Salt and Pepper, Northern Pickling).

humid_subtropical.notes: Long season allows succession sowing every 3 weeks. Heavy disease pressure — pick downy/powdery mildew resistant varieties; rotate beds.

arid.notes: Soil warms fast — direct-sow earlier than the calendar. Afternoon shade above 95°F prevents bitter fruit.

semi_arid.notes: Wind shreds large leaves — trellis with windbreak. Deep weekly watering preferred over frequent shallow.

entries.notes: One or two plants per household is plenty. Pick at 6-8 inches; oversized fruit pulls energy from new ones.

maritime.notes: Powdery mildew pressure mid-season — choose resistant varieties (Dunja, Anton, Spineless Beauty); succession sow 3-4 weeks apart so younger plants take over as older ones decline.

mediterranean.notes: Productive through dry summer with deep mulch and drip irrigation. Pick young (6 in) for best texture.

continental.notes: Fast and forgiving — one or two plants is enough. Pick daily once producing.

humid_subtropical.notes: Long season allows two waves (spring + late summer). Squash vine borer and squash bug pressure — kaolin clay or pyrethrin rotation; trap-crop with Hubbard squash.

arid.notes: Direct sow as soon as soil hits 60°F. Mulch heavily; afternoon shade above 100°F prevents bitter fruit.

semi_arid.notes: Wind shreds large leaves — windbreak helps. Drip irrigation preferred; mulch retains moisture.

entries.notes: Yellow crookneck, pattypan, and straightneck. Same care as zucchini.

maritime.notes: Same powdery mildew pressure as zucchini — choose resistant pattypan and yellow crookneck (Sunburst, Sunray F1). Cut-and-come-again — pick young.

mediterranean.notes: Extended season — succession sow every 3 weeks for fresh fruit through fall.

continental.notes: Fast and forgiving; multiple successions per season.

humid_subtropical.notes: Squash vine borer pressure intense — trap-crop with Hubbard, check stems weekly mid-July.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; mulch heavily; afternoon shade prevents bitter fruit and sunscald.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on large leaves; drip irrigation and mulch.

windows.notes: Long season — sow promptly after last frost

entries.notes: Cure 1-2 weeks at 80°F after harvest for storage. Includes Hubbard, kabocha, banana, buttercup.

maritime.notes: Short-season varieties only (Delicata, Sweet Dumpling, Buttercup, Red Kuri) — under 100 days. Cure outdoors in late summer sun before storage.

mediterranean.notes: Full season available — choose Kabocha, Butternut, large Hubbards. Deep mulch through summer drought.

continental.notes: Pre-warm with black plastic; choose 90-100 day varieties (Waltham Butternut, Honey Bear, Sweet Dumpling) for reliable orange-by-October.

humid_subtropical.notes: Vine borer + squash bug heavy — trellis bush varieties for airflow; rotate beds every 3 years.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory at flowering and fruit set. Afternoon shade prevents sunscald on developing fruit.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind exposure on large leaves — windbreak helps.

entries.notes: Resistant to squash vine borer. Cures and stores 3-6 months.
Eat within 2 months of harvest; flavor declines in long storage.

windows.notes: Time so harvest lands in October

entries.notes: Needs space — 50 sq ft per vine for full-size varieties.

maritime.notes: Large jack-o-lantern types marginal — choose pie pumpkins (Sugar Pie, Winter Luxury, Long Pie) maturing in 95-100 days. Cure outdoors before storage.

mediterranean.notes: Full season ideal — large field pumpkins (Howden, Connecticut Field) mature reliably.

continental.notes: Pre-warm soil; black plastic mulch; choose 90-100 day varieties (Spookie, New England Pie) for reliable orange-by-October.

humid_subtropical.notes: Long warm season; squash vine borer pressure peaks mid-July — check vines weekly.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; afternoon shade on developing fruit prevents sunscald.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once vines run; wind exposure on large leaves and stems.

windows.notes: Use peat pots to avoid root disturbance

entries.notes: Test ripeness by tapping (hollow sound) and checking the curly tendril nearest the fruit (browns when ripe).

maritime.notes: Marginal in maritime — choose icebox varieties (Sugar Baby, Yellow Doll, Blacktail Mountain) under low tunnels with black plastic mulch.

mediterranean.notes: Excellent — long warm season ideal. Trellis small types to save space; deep mulch retains moisture.

continental.notes: Short-season varieties only (Blacktail Mountain, Sugar Baby, Cal Sweet Bush); start indoors and transplant under row cover.

humid_subtropical.notes: Fusarium and anthracnose pressure — choose resistant varieties (Crimson Sweet, Charleston Gray); rotate beds every 3 years.

arid.notes: Ideal climate — soil warms fast, low disease. Deep weekly soaking; do not stress plants during fruit set.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind protection on young plants and developing fruit.

entries.notes: Slip — fruit detaches easily — signals ripeness.

maritime.notes: Marginal in maritime climate — cool nights stall fruit set. Choose short-season varieties (Minnesota Midget, Hales Best Jumbo) and use black plastic + low tunnels.

mediterranean.notes: Excellent — long warm season ideal. Deep mulch retains moisture; trellis to keep fruit off ground.

continental.notes: Use black plastic mulch + start indoors to extend the season. Northern varieties only (Earlichamp, Hannah's Choice).

humid_subtropical.notes: Long warm season favors melons. Bacterial wilt and powdery mildew are the main threats — rotate beds, choose resistant varieties.

arid.notes: Heat-loving — soil warms early. Direct-sow once soil hits 65°F. Drip irrigation; do not stress plants during fruit set.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Wind protection helps young plants. Trellis or pinch off late-summer flowers that won't ripen before frost.

entries.notes: Doesn't slip — pick when blossom end softens slightly and skin develops a creamy color.

maritime.notes: Marginal — choose Earlidew or other 75-day types under low tunnels with black plastic mulch.

mediterranean.notes: Excellent — long warm season produces sweet, full-flavored fruit.

continental.notes: Short-season varieties (Earlidew, Sweet Delight); pre-warm soil; harvest at slip stage.

humid_subtropical.notes: Bacterial wilt and powdery mildew pressure — choose resistant varieties; rotate beds.

arid.notes: Ideal — heat-loving with low disease; deep weekly watering.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant; wind protection on young plants.

windows.notes: From seed: 10-12 weeks before last frost
Sets and transplants tolerate light frost

entries.notes: Day-length sensitive: long-day varieties for North (>40°N), short-day for South (<35°N), intermediate for the middle.

maritime.notes: Long-day onions (Walla Walla, Patterson, Copra) for storage. Maritime springs work well; thrips pressure in dry summers.

mediterranean.notes: Short-day onions (Yellow Granex, Texas Early Grano) — only southern California reliably; intermediate-day for central coast.

continental.notes: Long-day onions; start indoors 10-12 weeks before transplant. Cold-store storage onions (Copra, Patterson).

humid_subtropical.notes: Short-day onions for southeast (zones 8+); plant Oct-Nov for May harvest. Pink root and thrips pressure.

arid.notes: Short-day for southwest; plant Oct-Nov for spring harvest. Deep watering critical at bulbing.

semi_arid.notes: Long-day or intermediate-day; wind exposure on tall tops; consistent moisture at bulbing.

windows.notes: Plant deeply in trenches; hill soil to blanch the shaft

entries.notes: Overwinters in zones 7+ for very early spring harvest.

maritime.notes: Excellent — overwinters easily; King Richard or American Flag for fall and winter harvest.

mediterranean.notes: Fall through spring; skip summer.

continental.notes: Start indoors 10-12 weeks; hill soil around stems for blanching; cold-hardy to zone 5 with mulch.

humid_subtropical.notes: Fall crop primarily; spring leeks marginal in heat.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; deep mulch; spring crop preferred over summer in low desert.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall tops; consistent moisture.

windows.notes: Plant cloves 4-6 weeks before ground freezes; harvest the following midsummer

entries.notes: Hardneck varieties scape in spring (cut and use) and store 6 months. Softneck stores 9-12 months but no scapes.

maritime.notes: Hardneck varieties (Music, Chesnok Red) prefer the cool wet maritime winter and produce excellent scapes. Plant 6 weeks before ground freeze; mulch heavily.

mediterranean.notes: Softneck varieties (Inchelium Red, California Early) suit mild winters; hardnecks may not vernalize properly south of zone 9.

continental.notes: Hardneck varieties only — they need cold to vernalize. Plant 4-6 weeks before deep frost; mulch 4-6 inches with straw.

humid_subtropical.notes: Softneck varieties — hardnecks rot in humid winters. Plant October-November; harvest May-June before summer heat.

arid.notes: Both types work but irrigation is mandatory. Plant in October; mulch heavily to moderate soil moisture.

semi_arid.notes: Hardneck preferred; mulch 6+ inches to overwinter. Wind protection and irrigation through dry winter critical.

windows.notes: Fall-plant for spring harvest in zones 5+
Spring-plant in colder zones

entries.notes: Each clove produces 4-12 shallots. Cure dry for storage.

windows.notes: Succession sow every 3 weeks

entries.notes: Harvest at pencil thickness for milder flavor.

windows.notes: Divide established clumps every 3 years

entries.notes: Edible flowers in spring. Cut to 2 inches to encourage tender regrowth.
Closer to leek than true garlic. Mild flavor, large cloves.

windows.notes: Succession sow every 2-3 weeks until 60 days before first fall frost

entries.notes: No transplant — beans dislike root disturbance. Inoculate with Rhizobium for best yields.

maritime.notes: Wait until soil hits 60°F to direct sow — cool soil rots seed. Avoid mulch until soil warms.

mediterranean.notes: Succession sow every 2-3 weeks for extended harvest through fall.

continental.notes: Fast crop — multiple successions per season.

humid_subtropical.notes: Long season; succession sow. Rust and Mexican bean beetle pressure — pick resistant cultivars.

arid.notes: Soil warms fast; mulch heavily; drip irrigation through summer.

semi_arid.notes: Wind dries soil; mulch and irrigate consistently to prevent flower drop.

entries.notes: Single sowing produces all season. Needs 6-8 ft trellis.

maritime.notes: Wait for warm soil; long climbing season works in maritime once established. Powdery mildew pressure — airflow on trellis helps.

mediterranean.notes: Excellent — long climbing season produces for months. Trellis 6+ feet tall.

continental.notes: Works but bush beans more reliable in short seasons.

humid_subtropical.notes: Long warm season ideal; trellis aids airflow against rust and mildew.

arid.notes: Irrigation critical at flowering and pod fill; afternoon shade extends production.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall vines — secure trellis with stakes; drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Needs warmer soil than common beans; rots if planted too early.

Harvest pods when full and bright green; window is brief.

maritime.notes: Short-season varieties (Beer Friend, Envy, Butterbean); harvest at full plump pods before turning yellow.

mediterranean.notes: Long warm season; succession sow for extended harvest.

continental.notes: Full season works; choose 75-90 day varieties (Midori Giant, Envy).

humid_subtropical.notes: Long season; succession plant. Soybean rust monitoring during humid late summer.

arid.notes: Heat-loving but moisture-sensitive at flowering — consistent irrigation critical for full pod fill.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall stems; drip irrigation.

windows.notes: As soon as soil is workable

entries.notes: Trellis 4-6 ft. Pick frequently for continued production. Bolts in heat.

maritime.notes: Extended cool season favors peas; sow in late winter for spring harvest. Powdery mildew on older plants — pick resistant cultivars (Sugar Snap, Cascadia).

mediterranean.notes: Strict spring + fall pattern; sow as early as soil works in spring; resume in fall through winter.

continental.notes: Sow as soon as soil thaws — one of the first spring crops. Trellis tall varieties.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short window — sow very early in spring; resume in fall (October) for winter harvest in zones 8+.

arid.notes: Sow much earlier than the calendar suggests (Feb in zone 8); summer impossible.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection — trellis well-anchored; consistent moisture critical at flowering.

entries.notes: Pick when pods are flat with peas just visible.

windows.notes: Overwinters in zones 7+

entries.notes: Excellent nitrogen fixer. Cook young pods whole, shell mature beans.
Drought-tolerant once established. Each pod yields 1-2 chickpeas.

windows.notes: Succession sow every 2 weeks

entries.notes: Cut-and-come-again — harvest outer leaves, inner keeps growing. Bolts in heat over 75°F.

maritime.notes: Cool maritime summers extend lettuce season into June-July. Slugs are the primary pest — beer traps, copper tape, or iron phosphate baits.

mediterranean.notes: Hot dry summers force lettuce into a strict spring + fall pattern. Sow earlier in spring; skip summer; sow heavily in fall through winter.

continental.notes: Succession-sow every 2 weeks until daytime highs hit 75°F. Resume in late summer for fall crop.

humid_subtropical.notes: Lettuce season is short — sow as early as possible in spring; switch to heat-tolerant varieties (Jericho, Nevada) for late spring; resume in fall.

arid.notes: Sow much earlier in spring (often Feb) and through fall/winter. Summer impossible without 50% shade cloth and constant moisture.

entries.notes: Romaine and butterhead are easier than crisphead in home gardens.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on small seedlings; cool-season hardy.

entries.notes: Mild mustard flavor, frost-tolerant. Excellent salad green.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24 hours before planting

entries.notes: Spinach-like leaves all summer; not a true spinach. Pinch tips to encourage branching.
Loose, rock-free soil for straight roots. Keep moist during germination (2-3 weeks). Frost sweetens fall crop.

maritime.notes: Excellent — cool soil produces long sweet roots. Loose well-drained beds essential; carrot rust fly under row cover.

mediterranean.notes: Spring and fall; mulch heavily through dry season if maintaining.

continental.notes: Cold soil sweetens fall carrots; mulch for winter storage in ground (Napoli, Bolero).

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window; fall crop more reliable. Root knot nematode in light soils — rotate beds.

arid.notes: Sow early; skip summer. Consistent moisture critical for clean elongated roots.

semi_arid.notes: Wind dries seedbed — cover until germination; consistent moisture for clean roots.

entries.notes: Each seed is a cluster — thin to one plant per 3 inches. Greens are equally edible.

maritime.notes: Productive cool-summer crop; greens harvestable continuously alongside roots.

mediterranean.notes: Spring and fall; mulch through dry summer if maintaining for fall harvest.

continental.notes: Both spring and fall crops; cold-stored beets last all winter (Lutz Salad Leaf, Detroit Dark Red).

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window; primarily fall crop.

arid.notes: Sow early; mulch heavily; afternoon shade above 95°F prevents zone-fading on roots.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on greens; consistent moisture.

windows.notes: Succession sow every 2 weeks

entries.notes: Spiciest in heat — best flavor from quick spring/fall growth.

maritime.notes: Extended cool-summer season; succession sow weekly. Daikon as fall crop for storage.

mediterranean.notes: Succession sow spring through winter; skip summer. French breakfast types fastest (25 days).

continental.notes: Fast and forgiving; multiple plantings per season; daikon for fall storage.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window; fall crop most productive. Flea beetle pressure on tops.

arid.notes: Sow early; skip summer; daikon in fall for winter storage.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on seedlings; consistent moisture for clean roots.

entries.notes: Long roots need deep, loose soil. Excellent for fermentation.

windows.notes: Use fresh seed — viability drops fast. Slow germination (3-4 weeks)

entries.notes: Frost converts starch to sugar — best harvested after first hard frost. Sap is photosensitizing; wear gloves.
Oyster-flavored root. Best after frost; can overwinter in ground in zone 6+.
Aggressive spreader — contain in a dedicated bed. Harvest after frost knocks back the tops.

windows.notes: Plant slips (rooted shoots) when soil is reliably 65°F

entries.notes: Cure 1-2 weeks at 80°F + high humidity for best storage flavor.

maritime.notes: Marginal — choose Beauregard or Georgia Jet (90-day fastest); black plastic mulch is essential to lift soil temps.

mediterranean.notes: Excellent — long warm soil produces large reliable harvests.

continental.notes: Short-season varieties (Beauregard, Georgia Jet); plant slips after soil hits 65°F; black plastic mulch; dig before first frost — frozen sweet potatoes rot in storage.

humid_subtropical.notes: Signature regional crop — long productive season. Sweet potato weevil pressure — buy certified slips; rotate beds.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; deep mulch retains scarce moisture. Irrigation mandatory at tuber sizing.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once vines run; wind exposure on sprawling vines.

entries.notes: Plant root cuttings angled in deep loose soil. Harvest after first frost. Aggressive — contain it.
Heavy water demand. Blanch by hilling soil or wrapping for milder flavor.

maritime.notes: Signature climate — cool wet conditions match celery's needs. Long harvest window (10-14 weeks).

mediterranean.notes: Spring crop only — needs 16+ weeks at <75°F; skip summer entirely.

continental.notes: Short window; start indoors 10-12 weeks early; mulch and constant water — celery never tolerates dry soil.

humid_subtropical.notes: Primarily fall — heat shuts celery down. Cutting celery (Par-Cel) as a tolerant substitute.

arid.notes: Extremely difficult — needs 70%+ humidity. Skip in favor of cutting celery (Par-Cel).

semi_arid.notes: Extremely difficult — wind desiccates. Greenhouse production preferred; or cutting celery as substitute.

entries.notes: Florence fennel — bolts fast in spring heat. Fall is more reliable.

windows.notes: Plant 1- or 2-year-old crowns 6-8 inches deep in trenches

entries.notes: Don't harvest year 1; light harvest year 2; full harvest year 3+. Productive for 15-25 years.

maritime.notes: Excellent — cool springs produce sweetest spears; rust pressure controlled by airflow. All-male cultivars (Jersey Knight, Jersey Giant) most productive.

mediterranean.notes: Needs winter chill — central/northern CA only; mulch heavily through dry summer to maintain ferns.

continental.notes: Heritage crop — beds productive 20+ years. Mulch heavily for winter; harvest stops in late June (don't over-harvest year 1-2).

humid_subtropical.notes: Needs winter dormancy — marginal in zones 9+. Rust pressure in humid summer — overhead watering avoidance.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; deep weekly soaking; mulch heavily.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall ferns — protect young plantings; consistent drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Don't harvest year 1; light pull year 2; full harvest year 3+. Leaves are toxic — only stalks are edible.

maritime.notes: Signature crop — cool wet conditions ideal. Harvest 8-12 weeks in spring; stop when summer heat sets in.

mediterranean.notes: Needs winter chill — central/northern CA only; mulch heavily through dry summer.

continental.notes: Heritage crop — productive for decades. Harvest stops with summer heat; cold winters favor reliable dormancy.

humid_subtropical.notes: Too hot for reliable production below zone 7 — heat shuts plants down by midsummer.

arid.notes: Too hot — skip unless at high elevation (zone 6-7 mountains).

semi_arid.notes: Possible at higher elevations; wind exposure on large leaves; consistent moisture.

windows.notes: Vernalize seedlings at 50°F for 10 days to trigger annual flowering in zones 4-6

entries.notes: Perennial in zones 7+, grown as annual elsewhere. Mulch heavily for winter.

maritime.notes: Overwinters easily; productive 4-5 years. Imperial Star, Green Globe, Violetta.

mediterranean.notes: Signature crop — coastal California is artichoke heartland. Year-round production possible.

continental.notes: Annual production from seed (Imperial Star, Tavor); start indoors 8-10 weeks early and vernalize seedlings (cold treatment) for first-year flowering.

humid_subtropical.notes: Annual production; bolts in heat. Plant in fall for spring buds in zones 9+.

arid.notes: Winter crop in low-desert zones (8b-9); needs cool winter season for bud development.

semi_arid.notes: Annual production; cool springs work. Wind protection on large plants; consistent drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Eat the blanched stalks like celery. Wrap stalks 4 weeks before harvest.

windows.notes: Plant in blocks of 4×4+ for wind pollination, not single rows

entries.notes: Pick when silks are brown and milk shoots from punctured kernel. Eat or freeze same day.

maritime.notes: Short-season cultivars (Trinity, Bodacious, Sugar Buns); cool soil delays germination — pre-sprout seed in damp paper towel or soak 6-12h before sowing.

mediterranean.notes: Long warm season; succession sow every 14 days for extended harvest.

continental.notes: Pre-warm soil with black plastic; block-plant (4×4 minimum) for pollination; succession sow every 14 days.

humid_subtropical.notes: Corn earworm pressure — mineral oil on silks deters larvae. Double crop possible (spring + fall).

arid.notes: Heat-loving; deep weekly soaking; mulch to retain moisture; irrigation critical at silking.

semi_arid.notes: Wind can lodge tall stalks — block plant for support; drought-tolerant once tasseling.

entries.notes: Pinch tops to encourage bushiness. Pinch flower buds for continued leaf production.

maritime.notes: Cool nights stunt basil — wait until nights stay above 50°F to transplant. Row cover or low tunnel speeds early growth.

mediterranean.notes: Long ideal season. Pinch flowers aggressively for sustained leaf production. Deep weekly watering.

continental.notes: Short season — start indoors on schedule and don't rush outdoors. One Wall-of-Water lets you transplant 2 weeks earlier.

humid_subtropical.notes: Direct-sow once soil hits 60°F; basil thrives in heat and humidity. Watch for downy mildew — choose resistant varieties (Prospera, Rutgers DMR).

arid.notes: Heat-loving — transplant a week early. Mulch heavily; afternoon shade above 100°F prevents leaf scorch.

semi_arid.notes: Wind dries leaves fast — windbreak and mulch. Drip irrigation preferred.

windows.notes: Slow to germinate (3-4 weeks) — soak seed overnight

entries.notes: Bolts second year — that's normal. Save seed or replant.

maritime.notes: Overwinters easily — year-round production. Slug pressure on tender leaves.

mediterranean.notes: Fall through spring; second-year plants flower (biennial habit) — replant annually.

continental.notes: Annual production; biennial in zone 6+ with mulch. Long taproot — direct sow or use deep transplants.

humid_subtropical.notes: Fall through spring; summer heat reduces flavor quality. Cutting parsley as alternative.

arid.notes: Winter crop; afternoon shade in late spring extends harvest before heat.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on tender leaves; consistent moisture.

entries.notes: Decorative; flat-leaf is more flavorful. Same care.

windows.notes: Succession sow every 2-3 weeks

entries.notes: Bolts in heat — let it go and harvest coriander seed. Slow-bolt cultivars exist for summer.

maritime.notes: Extended cool season — succession sow every 2-3 weeks. Bolt-resistant Calypso, Santo varieties.

mediterranean.notes: Fall-through-spring crop; skip summer entirely. Coriander seed harvest from bolted plants.

continental.notes: Sow early spring and again in fall; bolts in summer heat regardless of cultivar.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window; primarily fall/winter. Self-seeds prolifically once allowed to set seed.

arid.notes: Sow very early (Feb-Mar); skip summer entirely. Coriander seed harvest from bolted plants.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on tall bolting stems; consistent moisture extends harvest.

windows.notes: Doesn't transplant well; direct sow only

entries.notes: Self-sows readily. Companion to cabbage family — attracts beneficial insects.

maritime.notes: Extended cool season; succession sow every 3-4 weeks. Bolt-resistant Fernleaf, Hera, Bouquet.

mediterranean.notes: Spring and fall; bolts midsummer. Save seed for winter culinary use.

continental.notes: Fast crop — multiple successions before heat; saves seed easily for winter use.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short spring window — bolt-resistant Fernleaf, Hera. Primary crop in fall.

arid.notes: Sow early; skip summer; resume in fall.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall stems — secure with stakes if needed for seed harvest.

entries.notes: Greek oregano (subsp. hirtum) is the strongest-flavored. Hard prune annually to prevent woodiness.
Drought-tolerant once established. Replace plants every 4-5 years as they get woody.
Hardy to about 15°F — bring indoors or grow as annual in zones 6 and below. Slow from seed; start with cuttings or transplants.

maritime.notes: Overwinters in zones 7+ Pacific Northwest; choose Arp, Hill Hardy, Salem for zone 6 attempts. Sharp drainage essential — rots in waterlogged soil.

mediterranean.notes: Signature climate — drought-tolerant landscape shrub. Multiple cultivars (Tuscan Blue, Spice Islands, Blue Spires).

continental.notes: Bring containers indoors below zone 7; Arp survives outdoors to zone 6 with mulch and snow cover.

humid_subtropical.notes: Root rot pressure in humid summers — raised beds with sharp drainage and crushed-gravel mulch.

arid.notes: Heat- and drought-tolerant once established; native-like care; minimal water after year 1.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant; wind-tolerant; long-lived hedge or specimen plant.

entries.notes: Replace every 4-5 years. Prefers dry feet — don't overwater.
Tender perennial; grown as annual in cold zones.
True French tarragon doesn't set viable seed — buy plants or take cuttings. Russian tarragon is seed-grown but bland.
Self-sows aggressively — deadhead to control. Calming tea.
Bronze fennel is decorative + edible. Don't plant near dill (cross-pollinates) or beans (allelopathic).
Classic bean herb — plant near beans for flavor and pest deterrence.
Perennial cousin of summer savory. Stronger, more pine-like flavor.
English lavender is hardiest. Prefers alkaline, lean, dry soil. Prune by 1/3 after bloom — never into old wood.

maritime.notes: Munstead, Hidcote, and other English types (L. angustifolia) tolerate cool wet maritime; Spanish/French types rot. Sharp drainage essential.

mediterranean.notes: Signature climate — all types thrive (English, Spanish, French, intermedia hybrids).

continental.notes: English lavender (L. angustifolia — Munstead, Hidcote) hardy to zone 5; mulch for winter snow cover.

humid_subtropical.notes: Humid summers cause root rot — raised beds with sharp drainage and crushed-gravel mulch are essential. Phenomenal lavender (intermedia) most humidity-tolerant.

arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; native-like care; minimal water; do not overwater.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant; wind-tolerant; long-lived perennial — productive 10+ years with minimal care.

entries.notes: Celery-like flavor, perennial. One plant is enough — gets 6 ft tall.
Bolts fast. Likes light shade in summer. Subtle anise flavor.
Tender perennial — annual in cold climates. Pinch flowers for sweeter leaves.
Frost-tender. Bring potted plants indoors below zone 8.
Slow-growing tree to 30 ft, can stay potted. Hardy to about 15°F.
Aggressive runner — plant in containers or sunken pots. Cut back hard 2-3× per season.
Sterile hybrid — propagate by division. Same containment caveat as spearmint.

windows.notes: Fall planting for next-summer harvest in zones 6+

entries.notes: June-bearing for one big crop; everbearing/day-neutral for spread harvest. Renovate beds annually.

maritime.notes: June-bearing varieties (Hood, Shuksan, Totem) for the heaviest single crop; day-neutrals (Albion, Seascape) for season-long production.

mediterranean.notes: Day-neutral varieties produce 8+ months. Matted-row culture works well in coastal central CA.

continental.notes: June-bearing varieties hardy to zone 4 (Honeoye, Jewel, Earliglow); mulch heavily for winter root protection.

humid_subtropical.notes: Short-day varieties (Camarosa, Chandler, Sweet Charlie); replace plants annually due to anthracnose pressure.

arid.notes: Day-neutrals in raised beds with consistent drip irrigation; spider mite pressure in summer.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection; consistent drip irrigation; mulch heavily to retain scarce moisture.

entries.notes: Needs acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5). Plant 2+ varieties for cross-pollination. First real harvest year 3.

maritime.notes: Pacific Northwest is the blueberry heartland — acidic native soils, moderate temps, reliable rain. Northern highbush varieties (Bluecrop, Duke) thrive.

mediterranean.notes: Choose southern highbush varieties (Misty, O'Neal) for low chill hours. Acidify soil aggressively (pH 4.5-5.5) — most California soils are alkaline.

continental.notes: Northern highbush only (Bluecrop, Patriot, Northland). Mulch heavily for winter root protection.

humid_subtropical.notes: Choose rabbiteye varieties (Tifblue, Climax) — the only blueberries that handle humid heat and higher pH. Florida varieties for zone 9+.

arid.notes: Difficult — alkaline soils + low humidity stress plants. Container culture in acidic potting mix is the practical option; low-chill southern highbush in raised beds works in zones 7-8.

semi_arid.notes: Container culture or amended raised beds — most native soils are too alkaline. Wind protection critical.

entries.notes: Summer-bearing or everbearing. Trellis and prune annually. Spreads aggressively — install root barriers.

maritime.notes: Signature region — cool wet conditions favor raspberries. Choose virus-tested stock from reputable nursery; root rot in poorly drained soil.

mediterranean.notes: Needs winter chill — coastal central CA works; not for southern CA below zone 8.

continental.notes: Summer-bearing red varieties (Heritage, Latham, Killarney) hardy to zone 3; mulch heavily for winter.

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat and humidity stress; choose Dorman Red or Bababerry below zone 7. Anthracnose pressure heavy.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; afternoon shade prevents leaf scorch; cane borer pressure.

semi_arid.notes: Wind exposure on tall canes; consistent drip irrigation; cold winters favor reliable dormancy.

entries.notes: Thornless cultivars are easier to manage. Tip-pruning encourages branching.

maritime.notes: Marionberry, Boysenberry, Olallieberry — Pacific Northwest heritage cultivars excel.

mediterranean.notes: Thornless trailing types (Tribute, Boysen, Triple Crown) work well.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy thornless erects (Apache, Navaho, Triple Crown) for zone 5+.

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat-tolerant cultivars (Kiowa, Choctaw, Brazos); rosette disease pressure in southeast.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; trellis for partial shade on canes; afternoon shade preserves fruit quality.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection; consistent drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Banned in some states (white pine blister rust host). Check local rules.
Same blister-rust caveat as gooseberry. Trains well as cordon.
Most rust-susceptible Ribes — heavily restricted in white-pine states. Self-fertile.
Plant 2+ cultivars for pollination. Berries must be cooked — raw fruit is mildly toxic.
Trellis essential. Annual pruning is the most important task — leave 30-50 buds per vine.

maritime.notes: Wine grapes (Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Riesling, Müller-Thurgau) excel in cool maritime; table varieties marginal — too cool for full sugar.

mediterranean.notes: Signature climate — table and wine grapes both ideal. Phylloxera-resistant rootstock standard.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy varieties (Concord, Frontenac, Marquette, Edelweiss); winter protection with row burial in zones 4-5.

humid_subtropical.notes: Muscadine grapes (Vitis rotundifolia) — disease-tolerant native to southeast. European V. vinifera struggles with Pierce's disease.

arid.notes: Table grapes excellent (Thompson Seedless, Flame, Crimson); deep weekly soaking; afternoon shade on developing fruit.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; cold winters favor dormancy; wind training on young vines.

entries.notes: Cold-hardy varieties (Chicago Hardy, Brown Turkey) survive zone 6 with mulch. Often dies back in winter, regrows from roots.

maritime.notes: Cold-hardy cultivars (Desert King, Lattarula, Stella) and protected microclimates (south wall) needed in zones 7-8 maritime.

mediterranean.notes: Signature crop — multiple types (Black Mission, Brown Turkey, Calimyrna, Adriatic) all work.

continental.notes: Outside zone 6 keep figs in containers and overwinter indoors; or wrap and bury in ground for zones 5-6 attempts.

humid_subtropical.notes: Brown Turkey, Celeste tolerant of humidity; rust pressure on leaves — air circulation helps.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; deep weekly watering; shade cloth on developing fruit prevents sunscald.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind protection on young trees.

entries.notes: Need male and female plants (1 male per 6 females). Heavy trellis required — vines reach 30 ft.

maritime.notes: Excellent — cool wet conditions ideal. Choose male + female cultivars (e.g. Anna female + 74-49 male) for pollination.

mediterranean.notes: Works well in cooler mediterranean zones; needs winter chill.

continental.notes: Hardy Actinidia kolomikta or arguta to zone 4; vines need strong trellis (8+ ft tall).

humid_subtropical.notes: Fungal pressure in humid summers — choose disease-resistant cultivars; airflow on trellis.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; afternoon shade; deep weekly watering.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on vines; consistent drip irrigation; cold winters favor reliable dormancy.

entries.notes: North America's largest native fruit. Plant 2+ for cross-pollination. Tropical custard flavor.
Astringent until soft and fully ripe — usually after first frost. Native and very pest-resistant.

maritime.notes: Asian persimmons (Fuyu, Hachiya, Saijo) work in zones 7-8 maritime; ripen in fall before deep cold.

mediterranean.notes: Signature climate for Asian persimmons — long warm season produces large sweet fruit.

continental.notes: American persimmons (D. virginiana — Yates, Meader) for zones 4-6; Asian require zone 7+.

humid_subtropical.notes: Both species productive; American native to southeast — Eureka, Ennis, Yates.

arid.notes: Asian varieties; irrigation mandatory; deep weekly watering; afternoon shade on developing fruit.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind training on young trees; consistent drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Heavy fruit drop stains everything — site away from walkways. Self-fertile.
Hardy cultivars (Salavatski, Surh-Anor) survive zone 6 with protection. Drought-tolerant.
Must be cooked. Membrillo paste, jelly, or roasted. Self-fertile.
Nitrogen-fixing. Dioecious — need male and female. Berries are tart, vitamin-C rich.
Earliest fruit of the year. Plant 2+ compatible cultivars for pollination.
Nitrogen-fixing shrub. Tart-sweet red berries. Self-fertile but better with 2+.
Considered invasive in many states — check local restrictions before planting. Otherwise vigorous, drought-tolerant, lycopene-rich.
Repels nematodes — plant near tomatoes and beans. French marigolds are most effective for nematode control.
Edible flowers and leaves (peppery). Trap crop for aphids. Don't fertilize — too much nitrogen = all leaves no flowers.
Edible petals. Self-sows readily. Cool-season — fades in summer heat.
Edible cucumber-flavored flowers. Bee magnet. Self-sows aggressively — let some go to seed.
Mammoth varieties get 12 ft. Stake or wind-shelter big heads. Cut head when back yellows for seed harvest.
Pollinator and cut-flower champion. Pinch first set of leaves for branching. Powdery mildew prone — water at soil.
Don't fertilize — poor soil = more flowers. Self-sows. Beneficial-insect attractor.
Honey-scented mat — excellent ground cover and beneficial-insect attractor. Tolerates light frost.
Hummingbird and bee favorite. Mildew-prone — choose resistant cultivars (Jacob Cline, Marshall's Delight).
Beneficial-insect magnet. Drought-tolerant. Cut back hard mid-season for second bloom.
One of the best bee plants known. Also a cover crop. Reseeds.
Edible petals. Self-sows. Tolerates poor soil.
Heat-tolerant tall annual (4-5 ft). Good back-border companion. Self-sows.

windows.notes: Fall-sow for winter cover and spring nitrogen
Spring-sow as a short cover before warm crops

entries.notes: Nitrogen fixer. Mow or cut at flowering, before seed set, for nitrogen release.
Long-lived nitrogen fixer. Excellent living mulch in orchards and pathways.

windows.notes: Often mixed with winter rye for biomass + N

entries.notes: Strongest winter-hardy nitrogen fixer. Terminate before seed set or it volunteers everywhere.

windows.notes: Most cold-tolerant cereal cover crop

entries.notes: Excellent biomass and weed suppression. Terminate at boot/heading stage. Allelopathic — wait 3-4 weeks before planting tomatoes.

windows.notes: Quick summer cover; flowers in 30 days

entries.notes: Fast cover, weed suppression, pollinator forage. Frost-killed — terminate easily.

windows.notes: Fall-sown oats winter-kill in zones 7 and below — convenient mulch the following spring

entries.notes: Great winter-killed cover; no termination work.
Nitrogen fixer; often mixed with rye. Edible shoots in spring.
Biofumigant — chopped tissue suppresses soil pests and pathogens. Don't follow with brassica crops (shared diseases).
Deep taproot breaks compaction. Winter-kills in zones 6 and below.
Quick fall cover. Hard to terminate without tillage in zone 7+ — choose oats or rye if no-till.
Hardy to 15°F under row cover. Picks like a flat rosette of spoon-shaped leaves.
Prefers cool nights — best in mountain or northern climates. Rinse seed before cooking to remove bitter saponins.
Dual-purpose: young leaves are a heat-tolerant cooking green; mature seed heads are a pseudo-grain. Self-sows.
Plant unshelled or shelled raw seed. Pegs grow down into soil to form pods. Loose sandy soil and a 120-day frost-free season required.
Permaculture standby — deep taproot mines minerals; leaves chop-and-drop for mulch or steep into liquid fertilizer. Bocking 14 cultivar is sterile (won't spread by seed).
Edible licorice-flavored leaves and flowers. Top pollinator plant. Self-sows freely.

windows.notes: Cold stratify seed 4 weeks before sowing for best germination

entries.notes: Drought-tolerant native. Pollinator and finch magnet (leave seed heads through winter).

windows.notes: 7-4 weeks before last frost

entries.notes: Faster than slicers; ideal for short-season gardens. Indeterminate varieties need staking. Sungold, Sweet 100, Black Cherry are common cultivars.
Roma, San Marzano, Amish Paste types. Determinate growth - concentrated harvest for canning. Less prone to cracking than slicers.
entries.source: WSU Extension - Tomato Cultivars for Washington

windows.notes: 9-5 weeks before last frost; long season

entries.notes: Large fruit needs full season - skip in zones 3-4 unless using high tunnels. Brandywine, Cherokee Purple, Mortgage Lifter are heirloom favorites.
Oblong, denser flesh than cherry; less prone to cracking. Juliet and Santa F1 are reliable performers.
Pick green at 3-4 inches or red-ripe for sweeter heat. Stress (less water, less N) increases capsaicin.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Hot Pepper Production

entries.notes: Mild heat; fresh poblanos used for chiles rellenos. Dried equivalent is ancho.
About 5x hotter than jalapeño. Heavy producer; one or two plants is plenty for most kitchens.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Hot Pepper Production

windows.notes: 12-8 weeks before last frost; bottom heat helps

entries.notes: Long season; in cool zones use a south wall or container that warms quickly. Ripens orange to red.

windows.notes: 14-10 weeks before last frost; soil 80-85F for germination

entries.notes: Bhut jolokia. Wear gloves when handling fruit. Container culture in cool zones extends season.
Mild and sweet; pick yellow for pickling, red for fresh use. Good in short seasons.
Pick at 3 inches green for blistering. About 1 in 10 fruits is mildly hot - the lottery is part of the fun.
Mild long green chile; roast and peel. Dried red version is the New Mexico chile.
Dries easily for powder. Thin walls dehydrate quickly without a dehydrator.

windows.notes: Use peat pots - resents transplant disturbance

entries.notes: Edible skin; bush habit saves space. Best winter squash for short seasons (PNW gardens).
Japanese pumpkin; sweet, dense, dry flesh. Cure 10 days after harvest for best flavor.
Long vines need 6+ feet of space. Flesh strands when raked with fork after roasting.
Largest of the storage squashes (10-20 lbs). Bumpy blue-green skin stores all winter.
Summer squash; pick at 2-3 inches for tenderness. Bush habit, good in containers.
Pick at 4-6 inches before bumps form. More tender than straightneck.
Long fruits 18-24 inches; cure and store for winter. Pink Banana is the heirloom standard.
Ornamental and edible; sweet flesh. Distinctive cap-shape; popular for fall display.
Smaller, blockier than slicing types. Pick at 2-4 inches for gherkins, 4-6 for spears. Boston Pickling, National Pickling, Calypso.
Round yellow heirloom; mild, never bitter. Pick at lemon size before seeds harden.
Botanically a melon; ribbed pale-green fruit reaches 24+ inches. Burpless, no bitterness.

windows.notes: Trellis vertically for straight fruit

entries.notes: Long, slender, thin-skinned (Suyo Long, Tasty Jade). Trellising gives best fruit shape.

windows.notes: Fall crop performs best in mild fall climates

entries.notes: Fractal-shaped chartreuse heads. Behaves like cauliflower but more heat-tolerant.

entries.scientificName: Brassica oleracea x Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra
entries.notes: Aspabroc hybrid; tender stem and small florets. Cut-and-come-again for weeks.
Bitter italian green; harvest entire plant at first flower buds. Bolts fast in heat.
Chinese broccoli; eat thick stems, leaves, and pre-bolt buds. More heat-tolerant than European broccoli.
Flowering brassica; harvest at first yellow buds. Quick succession every 2 weeks for continuous supply.
Mild brassica green; whole plant cut at 8-10 inches.
Japanese mustard spinach; very cold-hardy, harvest into snow with row cover.
Green-stemmed baby bok choy; quick to mature, good for succession sowing.

windows.notes: Long season - start mid-summer for fall harvest

entries.notes: Brussels sprouts x kale cross; loose frilly mini-rosettes on a tall stem. Sweetened by frost.
Crinkled leaves, milder flavor than green cabbage. Frost-tolerant; flavor improves after cold.

entries.scientificName: Brassica oleracea var. capitata f. rubra
entries.notes: Stores 4-6 months in root cellar. Anthocyanin content rises in cool weather.
Conehead types (Caraflex, Early Jersey Wakefield); tender, sweet; small heads good for small gardens.
Same culture as green kohlrabi; harvest at 2-3 inches for best texture.
Falstaff, Red Bull. Slightly slower than green; sweeter after frost.
Upright heads more heat-tolerant than butterhead. Parris Island, Jericho, Forellenschluss.
Soft loose heads; Boston, Bibb, Buttercrunch. Bolts in heat - shade in summer.
Crisphead; needs cool steady weather to form tight heads. Difficult in hot summers.
Crispheads of European origin; bolt-resistant, good for hot summers (Nevada, Sierra).
Red-leafed mountain spinach; heat- and drought-tolerant alternative to spinach. Self-seeds prolifically.
Succulent, lemony flavor; high in omega-3s. Treats existing weed pressure as a feature.
Wild-foraged or self-seeded. Cooked like spinach; high in oxalate so eat in moderation.

windows.notes: Best as overwintering crop in mild climates

entries.notes: Native PNW green; cold-hardy, mild succulent flavor. Self-seeds in shade.

windows.notes: Use fresh seed - viability drops fast

entries.notes: Italian saltwort; needles look like rosemary, taste like spinach. Tolerates saline soil.
Edible chrysanthemum; pinch tips for hotpot greens. Bolts in heat.
Egyptian spinach; mucilaginous when cooked, slick texture. Loves heat, no bolting issues in summer.
Tendergreen; mild mustard flavor, more cold-hardy than komatsuna.
Caribbean leafy amaranth; cut-and-come-again. Tolerates extreme heat.
Red, gold, and tricolor leaves; ornamental and edible. Distinct from grain amaranth - bred for tender foliage.
Cultivated chicory grown like dandelion greens; less bitter when cool. Catalogna types.
Wild ancestor of chard and beet; perennial in mild climates. Salt-tolerant, good for coastal gardens.
Long slender fruit with tender skin; less bitter than globe types. Ichiban, Millionaire.

windows.notes: Best as fall crop - bolts in spring heat

entries.notes: Chinese heirloom; green skin, pink interior. Sweeter and milder than spring radish.
Elongated red-and-white roots; mild, crisp. Quick succession every 10 days for spring/fall crops.
Mixed-color packet (red, white, purple, pink). Good for kids' gardens and farm stand displays.
Mu radish; large, dense, mild. Used for kimchi and stews.
Trellis required; fruit can curl - hang weights for straight gourds. Use immature like cucumber.

windows.notes: Indoor production year-round; frost dates not relevant

entries.notes: Indoor tray-grown; soak black-oil seed 8 hours, weight tray for first 3 days for tall stems. Harvest at first true leaf.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Soak speckled or dun peas 8 hours; can re-grow 1-2 cuttings before exhausting.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Daikon, China Rose, or Sango varieties. Spicy, crisp; one of the fastest microgreens.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: High in sulforaphane. Mild flavor; use seeds intended for microgreens (food-grade, untreated).

windows.notes: Indoor tray-grown year-round

entries.notes: Soak hard red wheat 8 hours; harvest at 6-7 inches for juicing.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Red Russian gives purple stems; lacinato gives blue-green. High in beta-carotene.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round; needs warmth

entries.notes: Mucilaginous seed - do not soak. Germinates slowly; bottom heat helps. Strong concentrated flavor.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Peppery; harvest before first true leaf for tender texture.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Hot pungent flavor. Red Garnet gives purple stems for visual contrast.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Bull's Blood and Detroit Dark Red give intense crimson stems. Slower than brassicas - patience.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round; slow germination

entries.notes: Crack seed gently before sowing for faster germination. Concentrated flavor of mature cilantro.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round

entries.notes: Maple-curry flavor; soak seed 6 hours. Used in Indian cuisine, lowers blood sugar in studies.

windows.notes: Jar or sprouter year-round

entries.notes: Soak 8 hours, rinse 2-3x daily. Rinse thoroughly before eating to reduce E. coli risk; use food-grade seed.

windows.notes: Jar/sprouter year-round; weight for thick sprouts

entries.notes: Classic asian sprout. Weight produces thick crunchy sprouts; no weight gives slim sprouts.

windows.notes: Jar/sprouter year-round

entries.notes: Mild sweet flavor; high in protein. Same safety practices as alfalfa.

windows.notes: Jar/sprouter year-round

entries.notes: Use whole brown or green lentils, never split. Cooks tenderly, mild pea flavor.
Perennial spinach substitute; tolerates shade. Don't harvest year 1. Spring shoots eaten like asparagus.

windows.notes: Plant root cuttings or 1-year crowns

entries.notes: Salt-tolerant brassica perennial; force-blanch tender stems in spring under pots. 10+ year lifespan.

windows.notes: Fall planting establishes for spring greens

entries.notes: Top-set bulbils flop and root, walking the patch over years. Use bulbils as scallions or replants.

windows.notes: Propagate by cuttings - rarely sets seed

entries.notes: 5+ year lifespan; pinch tips to keep bushy. Sterile - propagate by stem cuttings only.
Pungent broccoli-like flower buds in spring; peppery leaves. Drought-tolerant once established. Can spread aggressively.
Native nitrogen-fixing vine; small starchy tubers strung along roots. Don't harvest year 1; year 2-3 onwards.

windows.notes: Plant top-set bulbils in fall

entries.notes: Babington leek; produces top-set bulbils like walking onion. Eat tender shoots in spring.

windows.notes: Plant bulbs in fall under deciduous shade

entries.notes: Native woodland onion of eastern US. Slow-growing - 5-7 years from seed. Sustainable harvest takes leaves only, not bulbs.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Forest Cultivation of Ramps

entries.notes: Coastal perennial with leaves tasting like oysters. Difficult to establish from seed; prefers sandy, alkaline soil.

windows.notes: Plant slips when soil is reliably warm

entries.notes: Most common orange-flesh cultivar; reliable producer with strong vines. Cure 7-14 days at 80-85F before storage.
Murasaki/Satsuma-imo: purple skin, pale flesh, drier and sweeter than Beauregard. Good for cooler zones with row cover.
Purple-flesh; needs longest season of common cultivars. Best for zone 8+ unless using black plastic and row cover.
Italian flat onion; small (1.5-3 in), sweet, ideal for roasting whole. Stores 4-6 months.
Italian heirloom; elongated red bulbs, sweet enough to eat raw. Long-day type for northern latitudes.

windows.notes: Overwinter for early summer harvest

entries.notes: Sweet PNW heirloom; very low pungency. Poor storage - eat or process within 4-6 weeks. Long-day type.
entries.source: WSU Extension - Walla Walla Onion Production

windows.notes: Plant cloves 2 inches deep, mulch heavily

entries.notes: Hardneck; rich complex flavor, easy to peel. Produces scapes in late spring (snap off for bigger bulbs). Stores 4-6 months.
Porcelain hardneck; large 4-6 cloves per bulb, white skin. Cold-hardy; one of the best PNW cultivars. Stores 6-9 months.
Hardneck; deep purple striping on white skin, reportedly the best for roasting. Chesnok Red is reliable.
Doesn't bleed - good for salads. Touchstone Gold is bolt-resistant. Sweeter than red types.
Italian heirloom with concentric red-and-white rings. Color fades on cooking - eat raw or roast briefly.
Cigar-shaped roots; uniform slices for canning. Heirloom Forono and Cylindra cultivars.
Cutting celery; hollow stems, intense flavor. Easier than blanching celery. Par-Cel and Soup Celery.

windows.notes: Plant root divisions

entries.notes: Sweet medieval root vegetable; dies back annually but the crown persists. Dig roots in fall, replant crown.

windows.notes: Sow 6-8 weeks before first hard freeze

entries.notes: Hardy red or white varieties; vernalizes over winter, harvested July. Good rotation crop after corn or beans.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Winter Wheat Production

entries.notes: Hard red spring varieties for bread flour; grown in cooler northern climates without winter survival.

windows.notes: Winter barley in zones 5-8

entries.notes: Hulless varieties (e.g. Streaker, Faust) thresh free of hull, easier for home use. Good gluten-free grain.
Most cold-hardy small grain; same species as winter-rye cover crop but harvested for seed instead of green-manured. Distinct from cover-crop usage.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Rye for Grain

entries.notes: Ancient hulled wheat; tolerates poor soil better than modern wheat. Hull must be dehulled before milling.
Oldest cultivated wheat; single-grain spikelets. Lower yield than modern wheat but tolerates marginal soil.
Farro; ancient hulled wheat between einkorn and durum. Used in Italian dishes; nutty whole grain.
Wheat-rye hybrid; combines rye hardiness with wheat yield. Good for marginal soils.
Drought-tolerant grain for hot dry summers. Birds love it - row cover or scare strips help. Distinct from foxtail or proso.
Shortest-season grain crop; gluten-free. Common bird seed cultivar; can be milled for human consumption.
Drought-tolerant warm-season grain; gluten-free. Sweet sorghum cultivars yield syrup; grain types yield flour.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Sorghum Production

windows.notes: Surface-broadcast tiny seed; do not bury

entries.notes: Ethiopian staple grain; smallest cereal grain in the world. Gluten-free; flour for injera bread.
Hard kernels for grinding into cornmeal or polenta. Heirloom: Painted Mountain (cold-hardy), Bloody Butcher.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Corn for Grinding

entries.notes: Field corn for masa, cornmeal, animal feed. Indentation in kernel as it dries gives the name. Soft starch easier to grind than flint.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Field Corn Production

entries.notes: Isolate from sweet corn (300+ ft) to prevent cross-pollination. Dry on stalk until kernels glassy, then cure 1+ months.
Dryland-adapted rice; no flooding required. Duborskian and Loto are short-season cultivars for temperate gardens.
Distinct from leaf amaranth; bred for protein-rich seed. Plume Berlandieri, Manna de Montana. Pre-Columbian Mesoamerican staple.
Bears 5-10 years from grafted tree. Requires 60-70 ft mature spread. Carpathian strain hardy to zone 5.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Nut Tree Culture

entries.notes: Native to eastern US; juglone toxicity excludes most companions. Bears 8-15 years from seedling (full crop ~15 years; daysToHarvest capped at schema max of 3650).
Bushes (10-15 ft) easier than tree forms. Plant 2+ cultivars for cross-pollination. EFB-resistant cultivars for east coast (Theta, Yamhill).
Use Chinese (mollissima) or hybrids for blight resistance. Plant 2+ trees for pollination. Heavy bearer once established.
Blooms early - frost damages crop in zones 7-low. Hall's Hardy works in zone 5-6. Most need pollinator partner.
Tap-rooted - difficult to transplant once established. Long season needs zone 6+. Needs Type I and Type II for cross-pollination.
Native eastern US; sweet rich nut. Slow to bear from seed (8-15 years; daysToHarvest capped at schema max of 3650). Distinctive shaggy bark.
Dioecious - need male and female. Hot dry summers required (low humidity). Best in zones 7-9 with chill hours.
Subtropical evergreen; frost-tender. Hawaii, FL, southern CA. Self-fertile but heavier crops with cross-pollination.
Native southwest US; drought-tolerant. Slow to first crop (15-25 years; daysToHarvest capped at schema max of 3650). Italian stone pine (P. pinea) for warmer zones 7-10.
Japanese walnut variant; heart-shaped easy-cracking nuts. Mild flavor, less juglone toxicity than black walnut.
Native to northern hardwood forests; oblong sweet nut. Threatened by butternut canker - source canker-resistant trees.
Chilean conifer; large edible seeds. Dioecious - need male and female. Very slow (15-30 years to first bearing; daysToHarvest capped at schema max of 3650). Novelty for collectors.
Female trees produce nuts inside foul-smelling fruit. Edible kernel used in Asian cuisine. Plant females downwind. (15-30 years to bearing; daysToHarvest capped at schema max of 3650.)
1939 standard cultivar; spine-free pods. Pick at 3-4 inches every 2-3 days for tender pods.
Red pods turn green when cooked. Ornamental in vegetable garden. AAS winner.
Edible calyces for hibiscus tea (sorrel drink); tart leaves cooked like greens. Day-length sensitive - flowers as nights lengthen.

maritime.notes: Too short — needs 100+ frost-free days at warm temps. Greenhouse only outside zone 9+.

mediterranean.notes: Works in warmest mediterranean inland zones (10a+); marginal elsewhere.

continental.notes: Skip — needs zone 9+ for reliable outdoor production.

humid_subtropical.notes: Signature crop — long productive season. Harvest calyces 7-10 days after flower drop while still tender.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; deep weekly watering.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind exposure on tall plants.

entries.notes: Eat young as squash, or leave to dry on vine for sponges. Heavy trellis required - vines reach 30 ft.

maritime.notes: Too short a season for sponge harvest — fruit may set but won't mature. Skip unless growing as ornamental vine.

mediterranean.notes: Full season works for sponge harvest — pick once skin is brown and rattles.

continental.notes: Start indoors 6-8 weeks before transplant; black plastic mulch. Sponge maturity marginal in zones 5 and colder.

humid_subtropical.notes: Signature climate — long warm season produces multiple sponges per vine. Trellis high (8+ ft).

arid.notes: Heat-loving; deep weekly watering essential through summer.

semi_arid.notes: Wind protection on sprawling vines; secure trellis well-anchored.

entries.notes: Bumpy green fruit (Indian/Chinese types differ). Eat immature; mature fruit too bitter and orange. Soak/salt to reduce bitterness.

maritime.notes: Marginal — needs 80°F+ days and warm nights. Greenhouse or hoophouse production for reliable crop.

mediterranean.notes: Works well in inland heat — coastal too cool.

continental.notes: Too short a season for reliable production outdoors.

humid_subtropical.notes: Signature crop — long productive vines through summer. Trellis 6+ ft.

arid.notes: Heat-loving; afternoon shade above 105°F preserves fruit set.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once vines run; wind exposure.

entries.notes: Climbing relative of cowpea; pods 18-24 inches. Heat-loving, drought-tolerant; pick at 12-15 in for tender texture.
Every part edible - pods, leaves, flowers, tubers. Day-length sensitive - good for tropical and southern gardens.
Drumstick tree; leaves, pods, and roots all edible. Frost-tender but root-hardy in zone 8 (returns from base).
Frost-tender; freezes back at 32F. Solo and Maradol cultivars; bears in 9-12 months from seed in zones 10-11.
Tropical evergreen; tolerates light frost when established. Pineapple guava (Acca sellowiana) for zone 8 substitute.
Hardier than true guava; flower petals also edible. Self-fertile cultivars (Coolidge, Nazemetz) for single-tree gardens.
Vigorous vine; trellis required. Purple types more cold-hardy than yellow. Maypop (P. incarnata) is hardy substitute zones 5-9.

windows.notes: Plant any time in zone 10-11; container indoors elsewhere

entries.notes: Plant the rooted crown of a fresh fruit. 18-24 month cycle to first fruit. Sucker plants from base extend production.
M. basjoo is hardy to zone 6 with mulch protection; not edible quality. Edible cultivars (Goldfinger, Raja Puri) need zone 8b+.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome pieces 2 inches deep

entries.notes: Lift and harvest after foliage yellows or before first frost in cooler zones. Container in zones below 8.
Same family as ginger; longer season required. Container culture in cooler zones; bring indoors in winter.
Wetland or upland strains. Always cook - raw corms contain calcium oxalate crystals. Grown for poi in Hawaii.

windows.notes: Plant 8-12 inch stem cuttings horizontally

entries.notes: Always peel and cook - raw roots contain cyanogenic compounds. Sweet cultivars safer than bitter (used industrial).
Climbing vine; eat tuber only. Foliage and seed pods are toxic (rotenone). Day-length sensitive.

windows.notes: Plant whole sprouted fruit at 45 degree angle

entries.notes: Squash-family climber; plant whole sprouting fruit. Tubers, fruits, shoots all edible. Perennial in zone 8+.
Apple-shaped, crisp pear. Plant 2 cultivars for cross-pollination. Shinko, Hosui, 20th Century are reliable.
Bartlett, Bosc, Anjou, Comice. Pick green-ripe and ripen at room temp. Plant 2 cultivars for cross-pollination.

maritime.notes: Excellent — Bartlett, Anjou, Bosc all work. Fire blight pressure increases with summer humidity — choose resistant cultivars where possible.

mediterranean.notes: Needs adequate chill — coastal central California only; not for southern CA below zone 8.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy (Ure, Patten, Summercrisp) for zone 4+; fire blight pressure heavy in zones 6-7.

humid_subtropical.notes: Asian pears more humidity-tolerant than European. If European, choose fire-blight-resistant Magness, Maxine, Moonglow.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; codling moth and pear psylla pressure — pheromone traps standard.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; consistent drip irrigation through summer.

entries.notes: Match cultivar to chill hours and zone. Most need pollinator partner. Dwarf rootstocks for small gardens.

maritime.notes: Disease-resistant varieties (Liberty, Enterprise, Williams Pride, Goldrush) reduce spray needs in wet maritime; scab pressure heavy on susceptible cultivars.

mediterranean.notes: Low-chill varieties (Anna, Dorsett Golden, Ein Shemer) for southern California; standard varieties for cooler coast and interior.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy heritage varieties (Honeycrisp, Macoun, Northern Spy, Cortland); extreme cold winters define hardiness ceiling.

humid_subtropical.notes: Low-chill resistant cultivars (Anna, Ein Shemer, Tropic Sweet); fire blight pressure intense — choose resistant rootstock (M.111, G.41).

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; codling moth pressure — pheromone traps and bags on developing fruit.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; wind training on young trees; consistent drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Smaller fruit useful for jelly and as universal apple pollinator. Dolgo and Centennial are productive culinary types.
Most cultivars self-fruitful. Annual pruning critical for fruit quality. Plant on slope to avoid frost pockets.

maritime.notes: Peach leaf curl pressure intense in maritime — choose resistant cultivars (Frost, Q-1-8, Avalon Pride) and apply copper sprays at leaf drop.

mediterranean.notes: Signature climate — full warm season favors peaches. Many cultivars work; choose for chill hours of your microclimate.

continental.notes: Cold-hardy varieties (Reliance, Contender, Madison) for zones 5-6; cold winters and late frosts define ceiling.

humid_subtropical.notes: Brown rot pressure intense — choose resistant cultivars (Carolina Belle, Contender); thin aggressively to improve airflow.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; deep weekly watering; codling moth and peach borer pressure.

semi_arid.notes: Late frost pressure on early-blooming varieties — choose late-blooming (Reliance, Redhaven). Consistent drip irrigation.

entries.notes: Smooth-skinned peach genetic variant. Same culture as peach; more brown rot pressure due to thin skin.
Blooms early - frost-prone in marginal zones. Manchurian apricot (Prunus mandshurica) hardy to zone 3.
Stanley, Damson, Italian Prune. Plums dry to prunes. Most need pollinator partner; Stanley is self-fruitful.

maritime.notes: Brown rot pressure in wet springs — choose resistant Italian Prune, Stanley, Mount Royal.

mediterranean.notes: Full warm season favors European plums (Italian Prune, Stanley, Damson).

continental.notes: Most cold-hardy stone fruit — reliable in zones 4-5 (Stanley, Mount Royal, Pipestone).

humid_subtropical.notes: Japanese plums (Methley, Santa Rosa, Burbank) more humidity-tolerant than European. If European, prune for airflow.

arid.notes: Irrigation mandatory; deep watering at fruit set; thin to prevent branch breakage.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant once established; consistent drip irrigation; late-frost protection on early-blooming.

entries.notes: Round juicy fresh-eating plums (Santa Rosa, Methley). Bloom earlier than European; frost-prone in zones 5.
Bing, Rainier, Stella (self-fertile). Net for birds at first color. Cracking in heavy rain at ripening.

maritime.notes: Split-fruit pressure from late-spring rain at ripening — choose Lapins, Sweetheart, Skeena for crack resistance.

mediterranean.notes: Signature climate — long warm dry summer favors sweet cherries.

continental.notes: Bird damage major — netting required at color change. Cold winters define hardiness ceiling (Bing zone 5).

humid_subtropical.notes: Heat and humidity stress sweet cherries. Sour cherries (Montmorency) more tolerant; if sweet, choose low-chill (Royal Lee, Minnie Royal).

arid.notes: Heat-tolerant once established; deep watering at fruit set; bird pressure heavy at ripening.

semi_arid.notes: Drought-tolerant; bird pressure; wind exposure on young trees — stake and protect.

entries.notes: Self-fertile; smaller and more compact than sweet. Montmorency for pies; North Star for short seasons.
Cherry-bush hybrid (Prunus x kerrasis); 5-7 ft shrubs. Romeo, Juliet, Carmine Jewel. Hardy to zone 2.
Chicago Hardy, Brown Turkey hardy to zone 6 with winter wrap. Distinct from regular fig; tree returns from roots.
