Black currant x gooseberry hybrid; thornless, larger fruit than either parent. White pine blister rust restrictions may apply.
Low-growing evergreen relative of cranberry; needs acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5). Cool-summer climates only.
Native eastern US shrub; very high antioxidants. Astringent fresh - best processed (juice, jam, baked).
Native shrub; berry like blueberry-almond hybrid flavor. Birds compete - net at color change. Saskatoon (A. alnifolia) is the prairie type.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Fruit Shrubs

entries.notes: Medieval fruit; pick after first frost and 'blet' (soften 2-3 weeks) before eating. Self-fertile.
Dogwood with edible fruit; tart cherry-like. Plant 2 cultivars for best yield. Yellow March bloom is ornamental.
Mountain huckleberry of the PNW; very slow to establish. Needs acidic soil and partial shade. Mostly wild-foraged.
PNW native evergreen; tart-sweet berries. Tolerates deep shade and acid soil. Florists also use the foliage.
Blackberry-loganberry-youngberry hybrid; sweeter than blackberry. Trellis required; thornless cultivars available.
Raspberry x blackberry hybrid; tart, used for jelly and wine. Trailing - needs trellis. PNW classic.
Scottish bramble; large dark red berries. Trellis required. Less hardy than loganberry but sweeter fruit.
Oregon-bred trailing blackberry; the gold standard for pies. Trellis required.
Trailing hybrid bramble; complex flavor between raspberry and blackberry. Disneyland heritage - originally from Knott's farm.
Asian Rubus; sticky red berries. INVASIVE in eastern US - check local restrictions before planting.
Sweeter and less tart than red currant; pearl-translucent berries. Banned in some states (Ribes restrictions).
Self-fertile; produces 2 crops in long-season zones. Brown skin with rosy flesh. Container culture in zone 6.
Hardiest aromatic citrus (zone 7 with protection). Aromatic peel and juice for Japanese cuisine. Self-fertile.
Most cold-hardy citrus; eat whole with skin on. Container culture in cooler zones.
Compact citrus; fruits ornamentally. Tart; used like lemon. Common houseplant.
Subtropical evergreen; flowers fall, fruits spring. Frost-tender flowers limit fruit in zone 7-8 transition.
Large-fruited Wonderful and Parfianka cultivars; needs hot dry summer. Distinct from cold-hardy types.
Chinese date; thorny tree, drought- and heat-tolerant. Li and Lang are common cultivars; bears 2-3 years from grafted tree.
Mulberry relative; raspberry-flavored fruit. Female trees only. Drought-tolerant, hardy. Slow to first crop.

windows.notes: Perennial in zone 9+; annual elsewhere

entries.notes: Use stem base. Divide clumps in spring. Bring indoors in zones below 9 to overwinter.
Aromatic herb of Japanese cuisine; mild basil-mint flavor. Self-seeds prolifically.
Used to color and flavor pickled umeboshi. Tastes more cinnamon-like than green shiso.
Mexican herb; pungent leaves used in bean dishes (reduces gas). Self-seeds aggressively - manage seed heads.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Mexican Herbs

windows.notes: Fresh seed only - sow immediately

entries.notes: Tall (6-8 ft); candied stems and seeds for liqueurs. Dies after flowering year 2 - lift and pot up to overwinter for year 3 if needed.
Sacred basil of India; clove-anise flavor. Used in tea (adaptogen). Rama, Krishna, Vana cultivars.
Anise-licorice flavor; purple stems and flowers. More heat-tolerant than sweet basil.
Bright citrus aroma; pinch flowers to extend leaf production. Mrs. Burns is the heirloom standard.
Subtle cinnamon-clove notes; ornamental purple stems. Mexican origin.
Anise-flavored marigold; substitutes for true tarragon in hot climates where tarragon fails. Yellow flowers in fall.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Mexican Herbs

entries.notes: Annual seed crop; harvest umbels at brown stage. Distinct from anise hyssop (different family).
Biennial; foliage year 1, seed crop year 2. Distinct from cumin. Used in rye bread.
Long warm season required. Harvest at brown seed stage; thresh and dry. Mediterranean climates ideal.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Spice Crops

windows.notes: Bolting cilantro becomes coriander - succession-sow for both

entries.notes: Cilantro grown for seed (coriander). Harvest umbels at brown stage. Distinct culinary use from leaf cilantro.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Herb Seed Production

entries.notes: Perennial herb fennel grown for seed (distinct from bulb fennel). Florence type for bulb; common type for seed/foliage.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Herb Seed Production

entries.notes: Same plant as dill foliage; let it flower for seed crop. Mammoth dill is best dual-purpose cultivar.
Use leaves (methi) and seeds. Nitrogen-fixing legume that doubles as cover crop. Maple-curry aroma.
Black seed; used as bread topping (naan). Distinct from love-in-a-mist (N. damascena, ornamental).
Indian spice; thymol-rich seeds. Used in Indian breads and lentil dishes.
Distinct cultivar group from existing summer-savory entry; finer leaf form used in Quebec cuisine.
Trailing form (Prostratus, Huntington Carpet) good for walls and containers. Less hardy than upright rosemary.
Cold-hardy Texas heirloom; survives zone 6 with good drainage and protected siting. Hardy rosemary for marginal climates.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Hardy Rosemary

entries.notes: Mat-forming groundcover thyme; less culinary use than common thyme. Walkable lawn alternative.
Citrus-scented thyme; gold variegated forms ornamental. Less hardy than common thyme.
True culinary oregano - much more flavor than common Origanum vulgare. White flowers (vs. pink in common type).
Hybrid between marjoram and oregano; balanced flavor. Less hardy than Greek oregano.
Purple foliage form of common sage; same culinary use, more ornamental.
Pineapple-scented leaves; brilliant red fall flowers (hummingbird magnet). Tender perennial; annual in zones below 8.
Cocoa-mint flavor; same aggressive spreading habit as all mints - contain in pots.
Fuzzy round leaves; apple-mint flavor. Variegated 'pineapple mint' is the same species. Contain in pots.
Mint family; cat attractant. Mild sedative tea for humans. Self-seeds vigorously.
Ornamental nepeta; lavender flowers, drought-tolerant. Cats less interested than in true catnip. Walker's Low is a standard cultivar.
Low-growing form of winter savory; rock garden use. Same culinary value as upright type.
Different species from West Indian (citratus); more cold-tender, used for citral extraction.
Used for leaves (Thai/SE Asian cuisine), not fruit. Container culture in cooler zones; bring indoors below 25F.
Cilantro substitute that doesn't bolt in summer heat; spicy peppery flavor. Tender perennial - overwinter indoors.
Indian cuisine staple; aromatic leaves added to oil for tempering. Not related to curry powder. Container culture in cooler zones.
Bitter culinary and medicinal herb; can cause photodermatitis - wear gloves. Host for swallowtail butterflies.
Mediterranean herb; minty-bitter. Pollinator magnet. Distinct from anise hyssop (different family).
Succulent fuzzy leaves; intensely flavored, much stronger than Greek oregano. Annual outside zone 9.
Andean culinary herb; anise-citrus flavor. Distinct from ornamental marigolds. Suppresses nematodes via root exudates.
Same plant as rau ram; alternate name used in some catalogs. Cilantro substitute for hot weather.
Medicinal root; harvest in fall of year 2. Sleep aid. Cats also drawn to it - some use as catnip alternative.
Medicinal flower (mild antidepressant in studies). Photosensitizing - moderate use only. INVASIVE in some western states.
Adaptogen root; harvest first frost of year 1. Drought-tolerant once established. Solanaceae family.
Mucilaginous root and leaf for soothing teas (sore throat, digestion). Original source of confection marshmallow. Tall (4-6 ft) ornamental.
American skullcap; nervine for anxiety. Distinct from Chinese skullcap (S. baicalensis). Native eastern US.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Medicinal Native Plants

entries.notes: Maypop; native eastern US, hardy passionflower. Mild sedative leaf and flower. Spreads by rhizome - contain.
Liver-protective seed (silymarin). Spiny - wear gloves. INVASIVE in CA and several states; check local rules.
Migraine-prevention herb; chew 1-2 leaves daily. Self-seeds aggressively. Daisy-like flowers attract beneficials.
Tall biennial with woolly leaves; flowers used for ear infections (oil infusion). Self-seeds aggressively; weed in many areas.
Common lawn 'weed'; leaves drawing poultice for stings/scrapes. Distinct from edible plantain banana.
Same species as ornamental yarrow; flowers/leaves for wound styptic and fever tea. White flowers most medicinally active.
Same plant as elderberry but distinct culinary use - flowers for cordial, fritters, immune-supporting tea.
Same species as ornamental calendula; resin-rich petals for skin balm. Resina cultivar bred for pharmacy use.
Same species as culinary lemon balm; medicinal cultivars (e.g. Quedlinburger Niederliegende) bred for high rosmarinic acid. Mild calmative.
Distinct from existing ornamental echinacea entry; harvested for root-tincture in year 3. Root-only harvest kills plant.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 8-12 weeks for germination

entries.notes: Native prairie species; reportedly more medicinally potent than purpurea. Slower establishment.
Edible root (gobo) and medicinal blood-cleanser. Deep tap root - loosen soil deeply. Avoid burr-stage seed heads.
Mildest tulsi cultivar; light clove flavor. Best for first-time tulsi tea drinkers.
Purple-leaved tulsi; spicy peppery aroma. Highest essential oil content of common cultivars.
Ayurvedic adaptogen; round leaves added to salads or tea. Likes moist shade. Container indoor culture in cooler zones.
Use gloves to harvest; cooking destroys sting. Tonic green high in iron and minerals. Spreads aggressively - contain.
Same plant as raspberry; distinct culinary use - leaf tea (uterine tonic). Pick before flowering for best quality.
Native lavender-flowered Monarda; oregano-thyme aroma. Same family as bee balm; better drought tolerance.
Insect-repellent herb; use externally only. Yellow button flowers. INVASIVE in some western states.
Bitter silvery foliage; absinthe and vermouth flavoring. Use internally only with care - thujone is neuroactive in high doses.
Edible flower; mild wintergreen flavor. Self-seeds prolifically. Pansy ancestor.
Edible petals; sweet wintergreen note. Larger flowers than Johnny Jump Ups.
English/true lavender for cooking (Provence, Munstead); lower camphor than lavandins. Don't substitute spike lavender or hybrids.
Tropical hibiscus petals are edible; mild flavor for salads and tea. Distinct from roselle (calyx use).
Hardy rose for hips - high vitamin C. Salt-tolerant, beach-suitable. Spreads by suckers.
Pick male flowers (no fruit behind) early morning. Stuff with cheese, batter and fry. Same plant as zucchini/summer-squash.
Buds and flowers edible; remove pistils for digestion. Tubers also edible. Distinct from true lily (toxic). Common orange ditch lily is most palatable.
Edible petals (clove flavor); remove white heel before eating. Carnations and pinks are same genus.
Edible blue petals; Same plant as cornflower (existing entry covers ornamental use; this entry covers edible-flower use).
Dog rose; the species classically grown for hips (vitamin C jelly, syrup). Strain seeds before processing - irritant hairs.
Used in Asian cuisine for petals (tea, salads). Same species as shungiku - but harvested at flower stage rather than leaf stage.
6-foot orange daisies; monarch butterfly magnet. Drought-tolerant once established. Torch is the standard cultivar.
Papery globe flowers; excellent cut and dry. Heat- and drought-tolerant.
entries.source: UGA Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Production

entries.notes: Fluffy lavender-blue flowers; long bloom season. Pollinator favorite, deer-resistant.
Small lacy-leaved marigold with edible petals (citrus-tarragon flavor). Lemon Gem, Tangerine Gem.
Pincushion flower; long-lasting cut flower. Bees love them. Successive sowing for continuous bloom.
Native scarlet sage; hummingbird attractant. Self-seeds; perennial in zone 8+.
Hummingbird and bumblebee favorite. Drought-tolerant, deer-resistant. Distinct from anise hyssop (same genus).
Tall airy clusters of purple flowers; butterfly magnet. Self-seeds; perennial in zone 7-8.
Native short-lived perennial often grown as annual. Cherokee Sunset, Indian Summer. Bee and butterfly magnet.
Plains coreopsis; native annual, drought-tolerant. Bee and butterfly favorite. Self-seeds.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 60 days for germination

entries.notes: Monarch caterpillar host. Spreads by rhizome - allow space. Native eastern/midwest US.
Clumping milkweed for moist soils; less aggressive than common milkweed. Monarch host. Pink fragrant flowers.
Drought-tolerant orange milkweed; tap root means transplant carefully or sow in place. Monarch host plant.
Texas native annual; cool-season bloomer. Mass plantings best. Self-seeds in southern zones.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Annual Flowers

entries.notes: Cool-season annual; pinch tips for branching. Tall types make excellent cut flowers (Rocket, Maryland series).
Spice-scented spike flowers; cool-season cut. Heirloom varieties strongest scent. Bolts in heat.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24 hours before sowing

entries.notes: Annual flowering vine; intensely fragrant cut flower. Distinct from edible peas - sweet pea seeds are TOXIC, do not eat.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Sweet Pea Production

windows.notes: Fall-sow for spring bloom; cold required for germination

entries.notes: Cool-season cut flower; tall spike. Annual delphinium relative. All parts toxic - do not eat.
Fennel-like foliage; lacy blue-purple flowers, ornamental seed pods. Distinct from N. sativa (kalonji).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cottage Garden Annuals

entries.notes: Tall (5-9 ft) cottage classic; rust-prone but worth growing. Self-seeds. Edible petals.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cottage Garden Plants

entries.notes: Biennial; rosette year 1, towering bloom year 2. Bumblebee favorite. ALL PARTS TOXIC - source of digitalis. Do not eat.
Tall blue spires; cool-summer perennial. Stake required. Toxic to humans and livestock - do not eat.

windows.notes: Scarify seed for better germination

entries.notes: Russell hybrids; nitrogen-fixer. Cool-summer climates. Russell, Texas Bluebonnet (L. texensis) for southern gardens. Seeds toxic to humans.
Nitrogen-fixing winter or summer cover; pair with oats for biomass. 4030 cultivar common in southeast US.
Nitrogen-fixing biennial/short-lived perennial. Frost-seed into winter wheat in Feb-Mar in northern climates. Edible flowers.
Tillage radish; deep tap root breaks compacted soil, winter-kills in zones 3-6 leaving channels. Distinct from edible daikon.
Tropical legume cover; 6 ft tall, fast nitrogen, suppresses nematodes. Frost-killed in fall - great rotation crop.
Massive biomass for soil-building; mow to 6 inches when 3 ft to encourage regrowth. Allelopathic to weeds. Frost-kills.
Warm-season nitrogen-fixer; Iron Clay is the standard cover-crop cultivar. Distinct from black-eyed pea (food crop).
Biofumigant; allelopathic to soil pathogens. Distinct from existing mustard-cover (Brassica juncea); add diversity to brassica rotation.
Fast warm-season cover; frost-kills. Also used as wildlife forage and bird seed.
Self-seeding annual clover for winter cover in mild climates. Buries seed in ground - regrows next fall on its own.
Less hardy than hairy vetch but doesn't reseed as aggressively. Cahaba White, Lana woollypod cultivars.
Distinct cover-crop usage of phacelia (existing entry covers ornamental). Top bee-attracting flower among cover crops.
Nitrogen-fixing legume cover; less biomass than peas but good fit for short-season rotations.
entries.source: Montana State Extension - Pulse Cover Crops

entries.notes: Egyptian clover; fast nitrogen for short windows. Multi-cut perennial in mild climates. Frost-kills in zone 6 and below.
Deep-rooted biennial; massive nitrogen and biomass year 2. Bee favorite. Distinct from sweet clover white (Melilotus albus).
Tall (5-7 ft) biennial. Most aggressive sweet clover for biomass. Mow before flowering year 2 to incorporate.
Forage and biofumigant rapeseed; deep tap root, biomass for spring tillage. Distinct from canola (food oilseed).
Mild-climate winter vetch; less aggressive than hairy vetch. Showy purple flowers, pollinator value.
Faster than rye to establish; less aggressive residue. Use for winter cover where rye is too vigorous.
entries.source: USDA NRCS - Small Grain Cover Crops

entries.notes: Dry-bean type; harvest at full pod maturity. Black Turtle, Midnight, Eclipse cultivars. Bush habit; 100+ lbs per 1000 sqft realistic.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Dry Bean Production

entries.notes: Mottled brown dry bean; semi-vining bush. Hopi Pinto, Othello cultivars. Drought-tolerant southwestern staple.
Large red dry bean; full-season crop. Always cook to destroy phytohaemagglutinin (raw beans toxic).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Dry Bean Production

entries.notes: Small white dry bean for baked beans/cassoulet. Yellow-eye, Vermont Cranberry are heirloom alternates.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Dry Bean Production

entries.notes: Borlotti; striking red-and-white pods. Use fresh-shelled (60-70 days) or dry. Italian heirloom.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Dry Bean Production

entries.notes: Mild large white dry bean; good for slow soups. Productive in northern short-season gardens.
Sonoran Desert native; extreme drought- and heat-tolerance. White and brown cultivars. Only legume that really thrives at 110F+.
entries.source: University of Arizona Extension - Tepary Bean

entries.notes: Dry soybean for use as soaked bean or soyflour. Distinct from edamame (existing entry); harvest at full pod dry.
Small green bean for soups (dal) and sprouting. Heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant. Distinct from sprout-mung-bean entry (use case).
Small red bean used in red bean paste (Asian sweets). Bush habit; good in northern short-season gardens.
Ornamental and edible vine; purple pods. Soak pods to remove cyanogenic glycosides; dry seeds REQUIRE long cooking.
Tropical perennial legume (annual in temperate). Used as toor dal in Indian cuisine. Fixes nitrogen aggressively; doubles as windbreak.
Climbing form of lima; King of the Garden, Christmas. Distinct from existing bush lima-bean entry.

windows.notes: Overwinter in zones 7+

entries.notes: Distinct culinary cultivar group from existing fava-bean entry. Aquadulce, Windsor types for fresh use.
Same plant as long-bean entry above; alternate name used in seed catalogs. Climbing; trellis 6+ ft.
Large white chickpea; the standard for hummus. Distinct from existing chickpea entry by cultivar group focus.
Small dark mottled lentil; holds shape after cooking. Le Puy is the AOC cultivar.
Split lentil; cooks fast (15 min) and disintegrates - good for dal and soups.
Tiny black lentils that look like caviar after cooking. Holds shape, mild flavor, premium price point.
Distinct from cover-crop field pea entry; this is harvested for split-pea soup. Trapper, CDC Mozart cultivars.
Green-cotyledon split-pea cultivar; nuttier flavor than yellow. CDC Striker, Montech 4152.
Distinct cultivar group from existing runner-bean entry; ornamental red flowers, hummingbird favorite. Fresh shelly or dry use.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions

entries.notes: Andean tuber; sweet crisp texture like jicama, zero starch (fructooligosaccharides). Treat as annual in zones below 7.
Andean tuber; cure in sunlight 1-2 weeks for sweetness (reduces oxalic acid). Day-length sensitive - tubers form after fall equinox.
Andean nasturtium relative; pungent root, cure to mellow. Vigorous vine; trellis required. Edible flowers and leaves too.
Black-skinned salsify relative; sweeter than salsify. Long taproot - deep loose soil. Self-seeds.
Climbing perennial; long underground tubers. Bulbils (aerial 'beans') also edible. INVASIVE in southeast US - check state lists.
Same species as burdock-medicinal; this entry covers the Japanese culinary use as long mild root vegetable. Takinogawa cultivar.
True African yam (NOT sweet potato, which is sometimes called yam in US). Always cook to destroy alkaloids. Climbing vine, trellis required.
Andean root tasting like celery-carrot-potato. Tolerates highland zones (cool nights). Plant offsets, not seed.
Distinct cultivar group from existing salsify entry; oyster-flavor root, deep loose soil. Mammoth Sandwich Island is standard.
Anthocyanin-pigmented heirlooms (Cosmic Purple, Purple Haze). Color leaches when cooked.
Yellowstone, Solar Yellow heirlooms. Mild flavor, retain color cooked. High beta-carotene.
Lunar White, White Satin. Mild parsnip-like flavor; good for picky eaters who avoid 'carrot taste'.
Knob celery; mild celery flavor. Brilliant. Tellus, Mars cultivars. Long season; PNW gardens excel.
Parsnip-flavored root; foliage edible too. Hamburg parsley. Slow germination - pre-soak seed.
Distinct flower-bed planting from existing phacelia (cover crop) and lacy-phacelia (cover) entries; ornamental beneficial-insect strip.
Cucumber-flavored leaves for salads. Cold-hardy, drought-tolerant once established. Cut back to keep tender.

windows.notes: Best as overwintering crop

entries.notes: Same plant as miners-lettuce (alt name). This entry distinguishes the temperate-overwintering use case from native PNW use.
Distinct from existing chive entry (Allium schoenoprasum). Flat leaves, garlic flavor. Edible white star flowers in late summer.
Distinct cultivar group from existing lemongrass entry; bred for thick tender stem bases used in Thai/Vietnamese cooking.
Violetta di Chioggia, Purple of Romagna. Distinct from existing globe-artichoke entry (cultivar group). Bud color is ornamental as well as edible.
Distinct from existing stevia entry; this entry covers leaf-harvest use rather than ornamental. Pinch flower buds for highest stevioside. Annual outside zone 8.
Pinecone-shaped flowers produce fragrant gel used as natural shampoo. Hawaiian and Asian heritage plant. Container culture in cooler zones.
Amish heirloom; potato-leaf foliage, large pink fruit with classic tomato flavor. Sudduth's Strain is the most flavorful. Skip in zones 3-4 unless using a high tunnel.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: Tennessee heirloom said to come from the Cherokee people; dusky purple-pink fruit, smoky sweet flavor. Crack-prone in heavy rain - mulch and water consistently.
entries.source: UT Extension - Tomato Cultivars for Tennessee

entries.notes: Italian DOP paste tomato; long thin fruit, dense low-water flesh, the canonical Neapolitan pizza-sauce tomato. Indeterminate - stake or cage.
Determinate paste tomato; concentrated harvest for canning. More disease-tolerant than San Marzano and easier in humid summers.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Paste Tomato Production

entries.notes: Tom Wagner cultivar; ripe at green-with-yellow-stripes (tang-sweet flavor). Looks unripe even when mature - go by gentle squeeze.
Russian (Crimean) heirloom; dark mahogany-purple fruit, salty-rich flavor. More heat-tolerant than most beefsteaks.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: 1930s West Virginia heirloom by M.C. Byles (paid off his mortgage selling seedlings). 1-3 lb pink fruit, mild sweet flavor. Heavy yielder.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: Wisconsin heirloom; oxheart-shaped paste tomato, sweeter and meatier than Roma. Indeterminate - long harvest window. Excellent for sauce and dehydrating.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: West Virginia heirloom; yellow-with-red-streaks 1-2 lb beefsteak, low acid, sweet melon-fruit flavor.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: Michigan heirloom; bright orange 1-2 lb beefsteak, low acid, mango-citrus undertones. Color matches a glass of orange juice.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: F1 cherry hybrid; intense tropical-fruit sweetness, the gold standard of cherry tomato flavor. Skin cracks when over-ripe - pick daily.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cherry Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: AAS-winning grape tomato hybrid; crack-resistant, disease-tolerant, very productive. Reliable workhorse for humid eastern gardens.
Once Guinness world's hottest pepper (~1.6M SHU). Wear gloves. Long season - container culture in zones below 7 lets you bring indoors at first cold.
Caribbean heirloom; relative of habanero with fruity tropical aroma. The pepper for jerk seasoning.
Trinidadian super-hot; small wrinkled red fruit with stinger-like tail. ~1.4M SHU. Gloves mandatory.
California cultivar; jalapeño-shaped but ripens red, fruitier and slightly hotter. Common in fresh salsas.
Galician heirloom; pick green at 1-2 inches for blistering. Like shishito, ~1 in 10 is hot - the famous lottery.
AAS winner; sweet thin-walled bell type, early-maturing for short-season gardens. Yellow ripening to red.
South African heirloom; small cherry-shaped peppers, mild sweet-tangy heat. Trademarked but seeds available as Juanita or Sweet Piquante.
Syrian heirloom; medium heat, fruity-cumin notes. Dried and crushed for the famous 'Halaby' pepper flake.
Italian zucchini heirloom; ribbed cylindrical fruit, denser sweeter flesh than common zucchini. Pick at 6-8 inches. Male flowers exceptional for stuffing.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Italian Heirloom Squash

entries.notes: Italian climbing squash; pick young (12-16 in) for summer use, leave to mature for winter use. Vine borer resistant - C. moschata immunity.
Venetian heirloom; warty blue-green skin, sweet dense orange flesh. Long-keeping winter squash. Traditional pumpkin for ravioli.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Italian Heirloom Squash

entries.notes: Australian heirloom; ribbed slate-blue fruit, dry sweet flesh. Stores 6+ months in cool dry conditions.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Heirloom Winter Squash

entries.notes: Australian-New Zealand heirloom; flattened blue-grey 6-10 lb pumpkin. Lower-water flesh, excellent for pies and roasting.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Heirloom Winter Squash

entries.notes: French heirloom from Eysines (Bordeaux); peanut-warty pink-salmon skin, sweet smooth flesh. The warts develop from natural sugars exuding through skin.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - French Heirloom Squash

entries.notes: French heirloom; deeply ribbed crimson Cinderella-style pumpkin. Eating quality good though looks more decorative.
Maine heirloom; cylindrical flat-on-top fruit looks like an overgrown zucchini. Sweet dry flesh, prized for pies; cures from green to orange.
Small (4-7 lb) New England pie pumpkin; sweeter and drier than carving pumpkins. Small Sugar and New England Pie are common cultivars.
Carving pumpkin; 10-25 lb fruit. Connecticut Field is the heritage standard. Stringy bland flesh - decorative use, not pie.
Pre-1864 American pole bean heirloom; meaty 8-10 in pods, classic snap-bean flavor. Pick young for tenderness; let mature for shelling.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Pole Bean Cultivars

entries.notes: Pacific NW classic; available in bush and pole forms (Blue Lake 274 bush; Blue Lake FM-1K pole). Stringless, sweet, the canonical canning bean.
Dutch heirloom bush bean; cream pods with purple streaks (color cooks out). Tender and stringless; triple-use as snap, shelly, or dry.
Pre-1840 American heirloom pole lima; large maroon-and-cream marbled beans, chestnut flavor. Slow start - long warm season required.
entries.source: UGA Cooperative Extension - Lima Bean Cultivars

entries.notes: Vermont heirloom dry bean; white kidney shape with maroon speckles, holds shape after long cooking. Bush habit. Excellent baked bean.
Indigenous southwestern dry bean revived from Anasazi-era cave caches. White with burgundy splashes. Lower oligosaccharides than common pintos.
entries.source: Colorado State Extension - Heirloom Dry Beans

entries.notes: Half-black half-white dry bean (yin-yang pattern); short-season favorite. Good for soups and chili - mild flavor, holds shape.
1700s Vermont bush heirloom; round burgundy-streaked white bean. Distinct from Italian borlotti / cranberry-bean entry; this is the New England cultivar group.
Modern selection (1980s); white with brown saddle markings. Bush habit, AAS-recognized. Holds shape and color in cooking.
Hidatsa (Northern Plains) heirloom dry bean; cream with brown shield markings. Drought- and short-season tolerant.
AAS winner; reliable bibb-type with thick juicy leaves. More heat-tolerant than typical butterheads.
AAS winner; bronze-red ruffled looseleaf. Slow-bolting - one of the best for hot summers.
Mini romaine; 6-inch single-serving heads. Closely spaced (4-6 in) for high yield per bed.
Looseleaf with deeply lobed oak-shaped leaves; cut-and-come-again. Available in green and red forms (Royal Oak Leaf, Red Oak Leaf).
Italian intensely-frilled looseleaf; deep red. Mild flavor, holds shape on the plate. Lollo Bionda is the green sister cultivar.
1840s American heirloom; pointed elongated leaves. Heat-tolerant, slow to bolt. Available in green and red (Red Deer Tongue, Matchless).
Austrian heirloom romaine; green leaves splashed with maroon ('speckled trout back' is the name's literal meaning).
USDA-developed romaine; the workhorse romaine cultivar for commercial and home use. 12-inch upright heads.
1902 Burpee heirloom; the cultivar that made yellow sweet corn popular (white was previously preferred). Open-pollinated; save seed in 100-ft isolation.
Late-season white sweet corn hybrid; the gold standard of southern sweet corn. Sweetness diminishes 24 hours post-pick.
entries.source: UGA Cooperative Extension - Sweet Corn Cultivars

entries.notes: 1848 New Jersey heirloom; latest-maturing white sweet corn, holds milk stage 7-10 days. Tall stalks (8-9 ft). Open-pollinated.
Carl Barnes (Cherokee) flint-corn selection; jewel-toned translucent kernels. ORNAMENTAL CULTIVAR — kernels are decorative not edible fresh. Use Painted Mountain or other flint corn for grinding.
Mexican Zapotec heirloom flint corn; emerald-green kernels grind into jade-green flour. Used for tamales and atole.
1890s Connecticut heirloom; 'shoepeg' kernels arranged irregularly with no rows. White, sweet, classic for canning.
Cornell-bred slicing cucumber; reliable, disease-resistant, the workhorse open-pollinated slicer. Marketmore 76 is the modern selection.
1880s heirloom pickling cucumber; vigorous productive vine. Pick at 2-4 inches for cornichons, 4-6 for spears.
Clemson-bred pickling cucumber; bred for downy mildew and bacterial wilt resistance in southeastern climates.
French heirloom melon; small (2-3 lb), pale grey-green skin, intense aromatic orange flesh. Slip from vine when fully ripe — short window.
Amish heirloom rediscovered 1980s; dark-green rind dotted with yellow 'stars' and a 'moon'. 25-40 lb fruit, sweet pink flesh.
1956 'icebox' heirloom; small (8-10 lb) round fruit fits in a refrigerator drawer. Earliest watermelon for short-season gardens.
French heirloom; round 1.5-inch globes. Best for heavy clay or shallow soils where Nantes types fork.
Pre-1870 French heirloom; cylindrical 6-7 inch root, blunt tip. The carrot Nantes hybrids descend from. Sweet, bolt-resistant.
Modern open-pollinated purple-skinned carrot with orange interior. Spicy-sweet, anthocyanin-rich. Color leaches when boiled - roast or eat raw.
1870s Massachusetts heirloom; 7-inch cylindrical root tapering to a blunt point. Tolerant of heavy soils and storage-friendly. Danvers 126 is the modern strain.
1840 heirloom; deep burgundy foliage as ornamental as the root. Harvest leaves at 35 days for salads, roots at 60.
1911 Boston-bred heirloom; flattened-globe shape, dark red, fast to mature. Tall Top strain has the best greens for braising.
Dutch heirloom white beet; sweetest of all beets, doesn't bleed. Good for picky eaters who associate beets with intense color.
Scottish 1887 heirloom; massive (1-2 lb) sweet globe onion. Long-day type for northern latitudes. Poor storage - eat or process within 1-2 months.

windows.notes: Short-day type for southern overwinter

entries.notes: Louisiana heirloom; pungent purple-red short-day onion. Excellent storage, distinct flavor. For zone 6 and warmer; northerners use a long-day red instead.
Italian heirloom; large globe yellow-skinned onion, intense storage flavor. Long-day type. Stores 6+ months.

windows.notes: Plant bulbs in fall; harvest summer of next year

entries.notes: Multiplier onion - one bulb planted divides into 6-12. Save largest for replanting. Hamburger or Yellow Multiplier are common cultivars.
Early summer leek; long white shanks, slim form. Best for fresh use; not winter-hardy unlike Bandit/Bleu de Solaize.
French heirloom winter leek; blue-green leaves, very cold-hardy (-15F under mulch). Harvest through winter.
Italian Tuscan heirloom; dark blue-green strap leaves, sweetest after frost. Used for ribollita and kale chips.
1885 Russia/Canada heirloom; oak-shaped leaves with purple veins. More tender than curly kale - good raw in salads.
Most cold-hardy kale (-30F under snow); blue-green flat leaves. Russian/Siberian frontier heirloom.
Vates strain; compact 12-18 inch plants. The classic blue-green curly kale, sweet after frost. Cold-hardy to -10F.
1890 Italian heirloom; small main heads (3-4 in) followed by 8-12 weeks of side shoots. Best heirloom for continuous harvest.
1950s UMass selection; cold-hardy fall-cropping broccoli. 4-8 inch heads, sweet after frost.
AAS winner; rainbow-stemmed chard mix (red, pink, orange, yellow, white). Both ornamental and edible.
1934 Burpee selection; thick white stems and dark crinkled leaves. Heat- and frost-tolerant; cut-and-come-again all season.
Heirloom red-stemmed chard; deep burgundy stems and veins. Less heat-tolerant than Fordhook but more ornamental.
1700s European heirloom; small pear-shaped yellow fruit. Mild flavor; very productive vines through frost.
British heirloom cherry; sweet-tart balance, crack-resistant. Trusses of 6-12 fruit; very heavy yields.
Modern selection; dark mahogany-purple cherries with rich complex flavor. Heat-tolerant.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cherry Tomato Cultivars

entries.notes: Anthocyanin-rich jalapeño; ripens green-purple-red. Slightly milder than green jalapeño but visually striking.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Specialty Peppers

entries.notes: AAS winner; cone-shaped fruit ripens cream-rose-red, mild heat. Very productive in containers.
Mexican Chilaca pepper, dried form. Long thin fruit, mild heat, raisin-cocoa flavor when dried. Foundation of Oaxacan mole.
Poblano variant ripening to chocolate brown; dried for mole sauces. Sweeter and earthier than ancho.
Japanese teardrop-shaped winter squash; sweet chestnut flavor, edible thin skin. 4-7 lb fruit. Vining habit.
Single-serving (1 lb) ivory-and-green striped winter squash; sweet flesh, edible skin. Bake whole and stuff.
Hubbard-type Sioux heirloom; teardrop-shaped, orange-red mottled skin. Sweet dense flesh; revived from indigenous seed banks.
Italian-Greek bush horticultural bean; cream pods with red streaks. Use shelly stage (60 days) or dry. Similar pattern to cranberry/borlotti.
Italian flat-podded snap bean; tender even at full size, distinctive bean flavor. Available in pole (Romano Italian) and bush (Roma II) forms.
Bush bean with deep purple pods (color cooks to green - good doneness indicator). Cold-tolerant, good for early sowing.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Snap Bean Cultivars

entries.notes: 1885 French heirloom butterhead; cold-hardy, plantable spring through fall. Red-tipped green leaves.
Mexican heirloom climber with grape-sized fruit looking like miniature watermelons; cucumber-lime flavor. Trellis required.
1894 Australian heirloom; round yellow fruit, mild non-bitter. Distinct from existing lemon-cucumber entry by selection lineage; more crack-resistant.
OSU-bred (1979) snow pea; flat sweet pods, eat young at 4-5 inches. Bush habit, no trellis needed for short cultivars.
1908 American shelling pea heirloom; 7-9 peas per pod, sweet and prolific. Dwarf habit (24-30 in), no trellis needed.
British shelling pea; uniform 9-11 peas per pod. Disease-resistant (downy mildew, fusarium wilt). The standard for freezing.
Romaine-type heirloom; green leaves with maroon speckles. Bolt-resistant, similar to Forellenschluss but tighter heads.
Looseleaf heirloom; bronze-tipped pointed leaves. Mild, slow-bolting, good for warm-summer gardens.
French heirloom batavian crisphead; red-blushed leaves, slow to bolt in heat. Excellent for summer salads.
Austrian-Italian heirloom romaine; same speckle pattern as Forellenschluss. Distinct cultivar group of cos types with mottled leaves.
Winter-hardy leek; pale yellow-green flag leaves. Stands through winter under mulch in zone 5+.

windows.notes: Best as overwintering crop in mild climates

entries.notes: 1840 French heirloom; cold-hardy red romaine for overwintering. Develops deepest color in cool weather.
Tall heirloom shelling pea (5-6 ft); deep purple pods (color cooks out, peas stay green). Trellis required.
Japanese heirloom; tapered orange root, heat- and humidity-tolerant. Best summer-crop carrot for southern gardens.
AAS winner; purple skin with bright orange interior. Distinct from existing carrot-purple by F1 hybrid lineage and stronger color.
Perennial variegated kale; cream-edged blue-green leaves. 5+ year lifespan if cut back annually. Propagate by cuttings.
Open-pollinated multi-color blend; less uniform than F1 'Bright Lights' but seed-saveable. Five Color Silverbeet is the heirloom strain.
Dave Christensen Montana selection; multicolored flint corn for short-season high-altitude grinding. Edible (unlike Glass Gem).
Hopi flour corn; deep blue kernels grind into purple-blue cornmeal. Drought-tolerant; traditional southwestern staple.
1845 Virginia heirloom flint corn; deep red kernels. Good for grinding into red cornmeal and bourbon mashes.
North Dakota Mandan flint corn; multicolored short-season grinding corn. Drought- and cold-tolerant.

windows.notes: Inoculate fresh-cut oak/hickory/ironwood logs in late winter/early spring

entries.notes: Drill-and-dowel inoculation in 4-6 in oak/sugar-maple/ironwood logs. First flush 9-18 months after inoculation; logs produce 4-6 years. Soak logs 24h to force flushes.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Forest-Grown Mushroom Production

windows.notes: Indoor straw substrate year-round; outdoor totem logs late winter

entries.notes: Easiest gourmet mushroom; fruits on pasteurized straw, coffee grounds, or hardwood logs. 14-21 day cycle on indoor straw. Forgiving for beginners.

windows.notes: Indoor year-round; cool-temperature strain (55-65F)

entries.notes: Cool-season pearl variant; deep blue pin caps fade to grey. Crisper texture than pearl, classic oyster flavor.

windows.notes: Indoor sterile substrate year-round

entries.notes: Eryngii / French horn / trumpet royale; thick stems with abalone-like texture. Substrate-bag culture; harder than pearl, needs sterile technique.

windows.notes: Indoor warm strain (75-85F); short shelf life

entries.notes: Warm-fruiting tropical strain; vibrant pink turning beige. Bacon-like flavor when cooked. Very fast cycle but spoils within 2-3 days of harvest.

windows.notes: Indoor warm strain (70-80F)

entries.notes: Golden oyster; cashew-citrus aroma. Self-clones aggressively from prior batches. Shelf life only 2-3 days.

windows.notes: Outdoor log inoculation late winter; indoor sawdust block year-round

entries.notes: Drill-and-dowel oak/beech logs (3-9 month spawn run); or supplemented sawdust blocks indoors (90-120 day cycle). Lobster-flavored; nerve-regeneration research in progress.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Forest-Grown Mushroom Production

windows.notes: Inoculate hardwood-chip bed in spring after soil thaws

entries.notes: King Stropharia; grows in hardwood-chip mulch beds (paths, garden borders). First flush ~6 months after inoculation; bed produces 2-4 years before refresh. Mild flavor.
entries.source: OSU Extension Service - Wood Chip Mushroom Cultivation

windows.notes: Bury inoculated oak stumps/log butts in fall

entries.notes: Hen-of-the-woods; long spawn run (1-2 years before first fruit). Oak stump or partially-buried butt-log culture. Adaptogen used in Japanese medicine.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Forest-Grown Mushroom Production

windows.notes: Inoculate dying oak stumps; rare to fruit reliably

entries.notes: Bracket fungus on oak; chicken-textured flesh. Foraged more than cultivated - cultivation success ~30%. Always cook (causes upset raw or undercooked).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Forest-Grown Mushroom Production

windows.notes: Indoor cool chamber (45-55F)

entries.notes: Cluster-form long thin mushrooms; commercial form is light-deprived (white) but feral form is brown. Sawdust-block culture in cold dark conditions.

windows.notes: Inoculate beech/oak logs; same protocol as shiitake

entries.notes: Japanese cinnamon-cap; gelatinous coating, used in miso soup. Fruits in cooler weather than shiitake (40-60F).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Forest-Grown Mushroom Production

windows.notes: Indoor sawdust block; long spawn run

entries.notes: Italian black poplar mushroom; tan caps with darker centers. Earthy nutty flavor; needs more patience than oyster.

windows.notes: Outdoor oak/maple log; indoor sawdust block year-round

entries.notes: Medicinal polypore; not eaten fresh - sliced and dried for tea. Antler-form (CO2-rich) or conk-form depending on culture conditions. Adaptogen with long use in Asian medicine.

windows.notes: Inoculate hardwood logs/stumps spring or fall

entries.notes: Medicinal bracket fungus; PSK and PSP polysaccharide compounds in cancer-adjuvant research (Japanese kanpo). Tea/tincture only - too tough to eat.

windows.notes: Indoor sterile rice/grain substrate; CO2 control essential

entries.notes: C. militaris (cultivable) substitutes for endangered C. sinensis. Adaptogen used in Chinese medicine; orange fruiting bodies grown on grain in jars under fluorescent light.

windows.notes: Inoculate compost/wood-chip bed in spring or fall

entries.notes: Inky cap; deliquesces (auto-digests to black ink) within hours of harvest - eat within 2-3 hours. Prefers disturbed urban soils. AVOID alcohol within 72h.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Wild Mushroom Cultivation

windows.notes: Indoor pasteurized compost; warm strain (75-85F)

entries.notes: Brazilian Portobello cousin; almond-marzipan aroma when cooked. Compost culture similar to button mushrooms but warmer.

windows.notes: Inoculate compost/leaf-mold bed in fall

entries.notes: Lavender-stemmed wood-decomposer; outdoor compost-bed culture. Always cook to neutralize mild GI irritants. Field-pickers must distinguish from purple Cortinarius species (toxic).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Wild Mushroom Cultivation

windows.notes: Inoculate sclerotia/spawn into apple/elm/cottonwood beds in fall

entries.notes: Notoriously hard to cultivate; outdoor beds with apple/elm/cottonwood debris and inoculated soil. Success rate <30%. Always cook (raw causes GI upset).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Wild Mushroom Cultivation

entries.notes: PNW native bramble; thornless soft fragile berries (pick directly into mouth or freezer). Thicket-forming, partial shade tolerant. Spreads by rhizome.
PNW native; pink flowers in early spring, yellow-orange-red berries. Less sweet than blackberry but ripens earliest. Shoots also edible peeled in spring.
Indian plum / oso berry; PNW native shrub with earliest spring flowers. Bitter dark blue fruit traditionally used by Coast Salish; more important as early pollinator forage.
Bearberry; native evergreen groundcover. Mealy red berries (bear food, traditional smoking herb mix). Drought-tolerant, acidic soil. Medicinal urinary use.
Native PNW/Eastern US allium; pink nodding flower clusters. Bulbs/leaves/flowers all edible, mild garlic-onion flavor.

windows.notes: Plant bulbs in fall after summer dormancy

entries.notes: PNW native bulb; staple food of Coast Salish/Nez Perce peoples. Slow-pit-roasted to convert inulin to fructose (sweet result). DO NOT confuse with toxic Death Camas (Zigadenus).
PNW evergreen huckleberry; small tart-sweet black berries. Florists also use leathery foliage. Acidic soil and partial shade required.
PNW deciduous huckleberry; transparent red-orange berries on green-stemmed shrubs. Often grows on rotting nurse stumps - mimic in cultivation.
Oregon state flower; tart blue-black berries (pectin-rich, jelly use). Yellow root contains berberine - traditional medicinal. Holly-like evergreen leaves.

windows.notes: Plant corms in fall after summer dormancy

entries.notes: PNW/Rocky Mountain spring ephemeral; tiny edible corms (potato-like). Indigenous food source; harvest sustainably (replant smallest).
Native eastern US persimmon (distinct from Asian D. kaki); smaller astringent fruit, sweet only after first frost. Dioecious - need male and female. More cold-hardy than D. kaki.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Native Fruit Trees

entries.notes: Native eastern US shrub; red drupe clusters used as tart spice (sumac-aid). DO NOT confuse with toxic Poison Sumac (Toxicodendron) which has white drupes.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Edible Plants

entries.notes: Native eastern US understory shrub; aromatic leaves and red berries used as allspice substitute. Host plant for Spicebush Swallowtail butterfly.
Eastern US native; aromatic leaves for tea (not a true fern - in Myricaceae). Nitrogen-fixer for poor soils.
Boreal native dwarf dogwood; clusters of mealy red berries. Cool-summer climates only. Acidic shade groundcover.
Allegheny chinquapin; native eastern US bush-chestnut. Single nut per bur (vs 3 in true chestnut). More chestnut-blight resistant.
Native viburnum (NOT a cranberry); tart red drupes used for jelly. Sweetest after frost. Distinct from invasive European V. opulus.
Native viburnum; sweet raisin-like blue-black drupes after frost. Tall shrub or small tree. Wildlife favorite.
Native southeastern US grape; tough thick skins, intensely fragrant flesh. Resistant to Pierce's disease and phylloxera. Scuppernong is the famous bronze cultivar.
Southeastern native; brilliant magenta drupes (jelly use, mosquito-repelling leaf rub). USDA-confirmed insect-repelling terpenes.
Only caffeine-bearing native US plant; toasted leaves brew like green tea. Despite scientific epithet 'vomitoria', leaves are not emetic at culinary doses. BERRIES (not leaves) are toxic.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Native Plants

entries.notes: Native eastern/midwest US perennial groundcherry. Distinct from existing ground-cherry (Physalis pruinosa, annual). Sweeter when fully ripe and yellow.
Eastern/midwest native dwarf cherry; tart drupes for jelly. Drought- and cold-tolerant. Western Sand Cherry (P. besseyi) is similar.
Native eastern groundcover; bright red double-eyed berries. Bland but persistent through winter; wildlife food and traditional medicine.
Native southeastern thicket-forming plum; small red-yellow fruit. Drought-tolerant, valued by wildlife and indigenous peoples (Chickasaw, Choctaw).
Western US native elderberry; powdery-blue clusters. Distinct from existing elderberry (S. nigra/canadensis); sweet enough to eat dried with little processing.
Sonoran Desert native; seeds yield wax (NOT oil) used in cosmetics. Dioecious - plant 1 male per 5 females. Drought-, salt-, heat-tolerant.
entries.source: University of Arizona Extension - Desert Plants

entries.notes: Native to Sonoran Desert; both pads (nopales, dethorned) and fruit (tunas) edible. Hardy O. humifusa form survives zone 4. Burn off glochids (tiny hairs) before handling.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Native Cacti

entries.notes: Honey mesquite native to southwestern deserts; pods grind into sweet caramel-cinnamon flour (gluten-free). Nitrogen-fixer.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Native Trees

entries.notes: Bears once after 10-30 years (not 100); hearts roasted for traditional indigenous food. Sap (aguamiel) ferments to pulque. Daysharvest cap at schema max 3650.
Wild ancestor of all C. annuum cultivars; pea-sized red fruits, intense brief heat. Sonoran Desert native; perennial in zone 9+.
Tohono O'odham food; flower buds harvested in spring before bloom, dethorned and dried. High in calcium. Nopalitos-like flavor.
Southwestern native; large green pulpy fruit (banana-flavored). Pollinated solely by Yucca Moth (Tegeticula). Indigenous food and fiber source.
Native midwest/eastern thicket plum; small tart fruit for jelly and ferments. Fragrant white spring blossoms; butterfly host.

Native cold-hardy stone fruit; very tart dark red drupes. Use for jelly/syrup/wine - too astringent fresh. Schubert/Canada Red are ornamental cultivars. Pits contain cyanogenic glycosides - cook fruit, discard pits.
Native eastern/midwest hazelnut; smaller nuts than European but EFB-resistant in eastern US. Hybrid Beaked x American crosses produce well in zones 3-7.
Lakota staple food (timpsila); deep starchy root, harvested with stick. Difficult to cultivate - mycorrhizal needs not fully understood. Sustainable wild harvest.
Native pioneer cherry of disturbed forests; bright red sour drupes. Indigenous fruit traditionally pounded with meat for pemmican.
Native eastern/midwest strawberry (one of two parents of cultivated F. ananassa). Tiny intensely flavored fruit; spreads by runners. Excellent groundcover.
Native Sichuan-pepper relative; numbing aromatic berries. Eastern US prickly-ash has weaker tingle than Asian Z. simulans. Toothache traditional remedy.
Premier hay/forage legume; deep tap root, drought-tolerant. 3-5 cuts per season. 4-6 year stand life. Distinct from existing sprout-alfalfa entry (use case).
Cool-season pasture/hay grass; the standard horse hay. Slow-establishing but long-lived (5-10 years). Bunchgrass habit.
Cool-season bunchgrass; shade-tolerant pasture component, pairs with red clover. Fast establishment.
Long-lived bunchgrass; toxic Acremonium endophyte in older cultivars (Kentucky 31) - use endophyte-free or novel-endophyte seed for grazing.
Quick-establishing cool-season grass; pairs with white clover. Distinct from existing ryegrass-annual entry.
Sod-forming pasture grass; very cold-hardy, drought-tolerant. Slower to recover from grazing than orchard grass. Good for permanent pastures.
Warm-season sod-forming grass; aggressive, drought- and traffic-tolerant. Sterile hybrids (Tifton 85, Coastal) for hay. Considered invasive in some northern zones.
Subtropical pasture grass; drought- and acid-soil tolerant. Pensacola is the standard cultivar for southern US.
Native warm-season tallgrass; biofuel/forage/wildlife habitat. Cave-In-Rock and Blackwell are common cultivars. Slow establishment (2-3 years).
Native tallgrass prairie dominant; 'turkey foot' seedheads. 4-7 ft tall; deep roots. Habitat restoration favorite.
Native shortgrass-prairie species; 2-4 ft, blue-green summer foliage turns copper in fall. Drought-tolerant; widely planted in restorations.
Native tallgrass prairie species; gold-tipped panicles, 5-7 ft. Co-dominant with big bluestem in restorations.
Native bunchgrass relative of corn; 7-9 ft. High-quality forage but requires careful harvesting (regrows from crown). Slow to establish.
Large white clover for pasture. 2-3x larger than standard white clover. Bloat risk in cattle - manage stocking. Distinct from existing white-clover entry.
Non-bloating legume forage; tolerates wet acidic soils where alfalfa fails. Yellow flowers, claw-like seed pods.
Deep-rooted forb for pasture mixes; high-protein, mineral-dense forage. Persistent under proper grazing. Puna II, Forage Feast cultivars.
Narrow-leaf plantain; deep-rooted forage forb, high copper/cobalt. Tonic 5-Star, Lancelot are sown into pasture mixes.
Forage cultivars (Purple Top, Barkant) bred for both root and top yield. Strip-graze in fall/winter. Distinct from existing turnip food entry.
Mangel/fodder beet; massive 5-15 lb roots for cattle/pig winter feed. Distinct from sugar beet (lower sugar) and table beet (much larger).
Non-bloating legume forage; condensed-tannin protein. Drought-tolerant, deep tap root. Pink flower spikes attract pollinators.
Non-bloating long-lived forage legume; rhizomatous, sod-forming. Drought- and cold-tolerant. Slow to establish (2-3 years to full stand).
Warm-season annual legume; reseeds. Pasture/hay component for southern transition zones. Acid-tolerant.
Quick warm-season hay crop; harvest at boot stage for best quality. German, White Wonder, Siberian cultivars.
Same species as forage plantain; this entry covers the herbal medicinal use (mucilage for digestive/respiratory tea, fresh leaf poultice for stings).
Native bog evergreen; needs acidic peat (pH 4.0-5.5) and constant moisture. Floods only at harvest, not year-round. Stevens, Howes, Pilgrim are common cultivars.

windows.notes: Sow in 6-18 inch standing water

entries.notes: Native North American aquatic grass; staple food of Anishinaabe and other indigenous peoples (manoomin). Distinct from Asian rice. Annual species.

windows.notes: Plant rhizome divisions in pond muck

entries.notes: Sacred lotus; rhizomes (renkon), seeds, and young leaves all edible. Zone 5+ outdoors with deep enough water for rhizomes to overwinter below ice. Aggressive in confined ponds.
Chinese aquatic sedge; sweet crisp corms used in stir-fries. Long-season subtropical. NOT the invasive water chestnut (Trapa natans).
Cool flowing-water cultivation (52-58F year-round). Streamside or shaded greenhouse with constant water. 18-36 month cycle to harvest. Most US wasabi paste is dyed horseradish.

windows.notes: Sow in shallow water or wet soil

entries.notes: Tropical aquatic green; cut-and-come-again. FEDERAL NOXIOUS WEED in US — illegal to plant or transport without USDA permit. Check state restrictions.
Native wetland plant; nearly every part edible (rhizome flour, spring shoots, pollen, immature flower heads). Vigorous - confine to a tub or designated bog.

windows.notes: Plant tubers 2-4 in deep in pond mud

entries.notes: Native arrowhead; potato-like tubers traditionally harvested by women toe-treading in shallow water. Coast Salish and Chinookan staple food.
Native pond emergent; young shoots and seeds edible (seeds taste like rice). Purple flower spikes attract bees. Ornamental wetland plant.

windows.notes: Plant in wet soil or shallow water

entries.notes: Asian aquatic celery (minari, seri); used in Korean and Japanese cuisine. DO NOT confuse with Oenanthe crocata (water hemlock — TOXIC). Source seed/starts only from edible-vetted suppliers.

windows.notes: Saline-tolerant — coastal/saltmarsh or saltwater-irrigated

entries.notes: Glasswort / sea bean / sea asparagus; succulent salty annual of saltmarshes. Pick young tips. Naturally salty - rinse before cooking.
Asian herb (dokudami, giấp cá); strong fish-cilantro aroma, used raw in Vietnamese fresh-roll plates. Highly invasive when established - confine in containers.

windows.notes: Inoculate calm fresh water in spring

entries.notes: Tiny floating aquatic; high-protein animal feed (chicken, duck, fish). Doubles biomass every 2-3 days. Emerging human-food crop (Mankai).
Floating fern with N-fixing cyanobacteria symbiont; traditional rice-paddy green manure (boosts rice yield 10-30%). Animal feed and biomass.
True indigo; leaves fermented to extract indigotin pigment for blue dye. Long-season tropical legume; nitrogen-fixer.
European blue-dye plant; same indigotin as Indigofera but cold-hardy. Year 1 leaves for dye; year 2 flowers and seeds. INVASIVE in western US - check state regulations.
Roots harvested year 3+ for red dye (alizarin). Sprawling vine - trellis or contain. Historic Turkish-red textile pigment.
Yellow-dye plant (luteolin); flowering stalks year 2. Lightfast yellow used for tapestries, Lincoln green (over indigo).
Native plains tree; heartwood and shavings yield bright yellow dye. Historic hedge species. Rot-resistant wood for fence posts.
Florets yield carthamin (red) and yellow water-soluble pigment. Distinct from oilseed safflower. Used historically for cosmetics and silk dye.
Cold-hardier than true indigo; the indigo of Edo Japan. Fresh-leaf vat dyeing (no fermentation needed) accessible to home dyers.

windows.notes: Plant tubers after last frost; lift before first hard freeze in colder zones

entries.notes: Spent blooms yield orange-yellow-rust dyes; mordant on wool/silk. Tubers hardy zone 7+; lift and store in zones 3-6.
Yellow daisy perennial; flowers yield gold/orange dye on wool. Distinct from Roman/German chamomile (tea use). Drought-tolerant, deer-resistant.
Mediterranean culinary sumac (the bright red Middle Eastern spice); leaves and bark yield brown-tan tannin dyes. Distinct from native staghorn sumac.
Central American tree; heartwood yields haematoxylin (deep purple/black dye). Historic Mayan textile pigment. Slow tropical timber.
North African shrub; leaves dried and powdered for orange-red hair/skin/textile dye (lawsone). Container culture in cool zones.
Tropical shrub; bixin from seed coats colors cheese, butter, rice. Indigenous Amazonian body paint and food coloring (achiote). Container culture only outside zone 10.
Bright yellow shrub legume; flowers yield strong yellow dye. Nitrogen-fixer for poor soils. Less invasive than Scotch broom.
Tropical American mulberry-family tree; heartwood yields lightfast yellow-gold dye. Wood and sawdust used historically; closely related to osage orange.
Linen-fiber cultivar group; tall thin stems for retting. Distinct from oilseed-flax cultivars. Pull whole plant (don't cut) to preserve stem length.
Federally legal in US since 2018 Farm Bill if THC <0.3%. STATE LICENSE typically required for commercial growers. Grain, fiber, and dual-purpose cultivars exist.
entries.source: USDA AMS - Domestic Hemp Production Program

entries.notes: Upland cotton; long warm season. Some southern states REGULATE/PROHIBIT home growing for boll weevil eradication - check state ag department first.
entries.source: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension - Cotton Production

entries.notes: Asian nettle-family fiber; 2-3 cuttings per year once established. Stronger than cotton, silk-like sheen. Stings absent unlike true nettle.
African hibiscus relative; 8-15 ft annual. Bast fiber for paper, rope, geotextiles. Faster CO2 sequestration per acre than pine timber.
Same species as molokhia (food-use entry); this entry covers the bast-fiber use. Burlap, hessian, twine. Tropical/subtropical only.
Drought-tolerant agave grown for stiff leaf fiber (rope, twine, baja blankets). Plants live 7-10 years; once-bearing flower stalk signals end-of-life.
Largest temperate timber bamboo; 40-70 ft canes. Source for bamboo flooring, textile rayon. Running rhizome - install root barriers. Edible spring shoots.
Same species as nettle-stinging (food entry); this entry covers bast-fiber use. European traditional textile (nettle linen). Retting protocol similar to flax.
Same species as milkweed-common (pollinator entry); this entry covers seedfloss use (life vest stuffing during WWII; insulation). Bast fiber from stems.

windows.notes: Scarify seed for germination

entries.notes: Huang qi; immune-tonic root harvested year 4-5. Not the toxic locoweed Astragalus species - cultivated milkvetch is safe. Slow but long-lived.
Arctic adaptogen; rose-scented rhizome. Very cold-hardy, requires cool summers. Harvest year 4-5. Wild populations overharvested - cultivate from divisions.
Five-flavor berry; climbing vine. Dioecious - need male and female. Used in TCM as adaptogen. Berries combine sweet/sour/salty/bitter/pungent.
Siberian ginseng (NOT a true ginseng - different family). Adaptogen root harvested year 5+. Cold-hardy, shade-tolerant.
King of bitters; Indian/Chinese herb for immune support. Annual outside zone 9. Greenhouse for cool zones. Intensely bitter aerial parts harvested at flowering.
Amazonian climbing vine; thorned (the 'claws'). Inner bark used for immune/anti-inflammatory tea. Wild stocks endangered - cultivate from licensed nursery stock.
Southern African desert plant; secondary tubers harvested year 3+. Anti-inflammatory for joint pain. Not native US Proboscidea ('devil's claw' = unicorn plant).
Native Florida palm; berries harvested for prostate/urinary support. Slow to bear; protected — wild harvest requires permits in Florida.
Native eastern woodland; rhizomes for menopause/women's health (year 4+ harvest). Wild populations declining - forest-cultivate, don't wildcraft.
Native eastern woodland; root historically used for women's health, but POTENT — can cause cardiac issues. Trained herbalist use only.
Native woodland herb; berberine-rich rhizome for digestive/skin use. CITES-protected; wild harvest restricted. Forest farming under hardwood canopy is the ethical path.
Native woodland spring ephemeral; orange-red rhizome sap historic dye and folk skin treatment. CAUSTIC - black salve use can cause severe necrosis; external use only with care.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Forest Medicinals

entries.notes: Native eastern US vine; rhizome contains diosgenin (precursor for cortisone synthesis). Distinct from edible Asian yams - this species is medicinal/inedible raw.
entries.source: Penn State Extension - Native Medicinal Plants

entries.notes: Mediterranean shrub; berries used for women's hormonal balance (PMS). Lavender flower spikes attract pollinators. Drought-tolerant, deer-resistant.

windows.notes: Cold stratify; sow fresh seed in fall

entries.notes: Chinese angelica; root harvested year 2 for blood-tonic use in TCM. Distinct from European angelica (A. archangelica). Cool moist conditions.
Indian asparagus; tuberous roots used in Ayurveda for women's health. Climbing/scrambling habit, thorned. Container culture for cooler zones.

windows.notes: Plant in saturated soil or shallow water

entries.notes: Aquatic creeping herb; Ayurvedic memory/cognition tonic. Indoor aquarium-style containers in cooler zones. Distinct from Centella asiatica (also called brahmi).
Southern China climbing vine; gentle adaptogenic tea ('herb of immortality' in southern provinces with high centenarian rates). Tolerates partial shade.
California state flower; mild sedative tea. Self-seeds prolifically. Drought-tolerant native; sleeps with closed petals.
Original natural source of salicylic acid (gave aspirin its name; spirin from Spirea ulmaria). Almond-vanilla-scented summer flowers; flu/headache tea use.
Bark contains salicin — body metabolizes to salicylic acid (mild aspirin-like effect). Coppice annually for sustainable bark harvest. Same genus as basket willow.
Alpine European daisy; flowers infused in oil/salve for bruises, sprains. EXTERNAL USE ONLY (toxic if ingested). Sandy acidic soil; cool summers.
Native eastern US shrub; bark distillation yields the famous astringent. Yellow ribbon-flowers in late fall (after leaves drop). Understory.
South American holly; roasted leaves brewed for caffeinated mate tea. Container culture in cool zones. Slow to first commercial harvest.
Pacific Islander root for ceremonial relaxant beverage. Hawaii (zone 11) outdoor; container/greenhouse elsewhere. 3-5 years to harvestable rhizome.

Southeast Asian tree; leaves contain mitragynine (opioid-like alkaloid). LEGALITY VARIES BY US STATE — banned in AL, AR, IN, RI, VT, WI; restricted in others (county/city level). DEA listed as Drug of Concern but not federally scheduled. Check local law before planting or processing.
entries.source: USDA NRCS - Tropical Plants (legality reference: state regulatory code)

entries.notes: Mexican shrub; aromatic leaves brewed for tea (traditional aphrodisiac/mood-lift). Drought-tolerant. Container culture in zones below 9.
Amazonian tree; root/bark used for energy and libido. Sustainable cultivation rare; most market is wild-harvested. Container in cooler zones.
Indian tobacco; respiratory/anti-spasmodic herb. POTENT — overdose causes nausea/vomiting (the Latin epithet 'inflata' refers to inflated seedpods). Trained use only.
Mediterranean herb; bitter aerial parts for digestive tea, traditional galactagogue. Spiny - wear gloves. Self-seeds; remove flowerheads before set.
Aromatic perennial; dream herb (smudge, tea, pillow). INVASIVE in eastern US — confine in containers. Spreads aggressively by rhizome.
Native eastern wetland; aerial parts for fever/flu tea. Distinctive perfoliate leaves (stem pierces single fused-pair leaves). Pyrrolizidine alkaloid concerns at high doses.
European biennial lettuce; milky sap (lactucarium) is a mild sedative/analgesic. Distinct from edible Lactuca sativa. Bitter taste; smoke or tincture.

windows.notes: Cold stratify seed for germination

entries.notes: Native eastern wetland; nervine for tension and headaches. Tall purple-flower spikes; bee favorite. Wet to medium soils.
European mint-family perennial; cardiac/women's-health tonic. Bitter aerial parts; tincture preferred. Self-seeds aggressively; pinch flowers before set.
California chaparral shrub; sticky aromatic leaves for respiratory tea (asthma/cough). Drought-tolerant. Indigenous Spanish missionaries' 'holy herb.'
Cosmopolitan mint-family perennial; mild astringent for wounds and sore throat. Often appears spontaneously in lawns - manage rather than weed.
Native eastern woodland; rhizome for joint/connective-tissue support (year 4+ harvest). Arching stems with paired leaves. Don't confuse with toxic False Solomon's Seal (Maianthemum).
Sticky-leaf annual; lymphatic tonic tea/tincture (use fresh — dried loses activity). Velcro-like burrs cling to clothing.
European herb of historic use; tannic astringent for digestive/skin issues. Yellow flower spikes; clinging hooked seeds.

windows.notes: Cold stratify seed for germination

entries.notes: Native eastern US; white tubular flowers in early summer. 'Husker Red' is the AAS-winning burgundy-foliage cultivar. Bumblebee favorite.
Western US native; brilliant red tubular flowers, hummingbird magnet. Drought-tolerant for southwestern xeriscape gardens.
Western US native; deep blue-purple tubular flowers. Drought-tolerant; the easiest penstemon for inland mountain gardens.
Native moist-meadow perennial; gold-orange daisy flowers, late summer-fall bloom. Misnamed - doesn't cause hayfever (uses pollinators not wind).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Pollinator Plants

entries.notes: Compact (3 ft) hybrid; orange-red-yellow speckled daisies. Long bloom (mid-summer through frost). Pinch in spring for bushier habit.
Native prairie goldenrod; flat-topped flower clusters. Late-season pollinator essential. Doesn't cause hayfever (often blamed; ragweed is the actual culprit).
Cultivar with arching gold flower sprays; less aggressive than wild goldenrods. Mt. Cuba 2008 pollinator-attractor trial winner.
Tall (4-6 ft) native; deep purple/pink fall daisies. Critical late-season nectar for migrating monarchs. 'Purple Dome' is a compact cultivar.
Native eastern US; many cultivars available (Wood's Pink, Wood's Blue). Pinch in early summer for bushier autumn bloom. Mildew-prone in poor air circulation.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Native Pollinator Plants

entries.notes: Drought-tolerant native; aromatic foliage when crushed. October Skies, Raydon's Favorite are top cultivars. Latest-blooming aster.
Native woodland aster; sky-blue clouds of flowers in part shade. Self-seeds without becoming weedy. Heart-shaped basal leaves.

windows.notes: Plant corms 4-6 in deep

entries.notes: Native prairie corm; purple bottle-brush flower spikes. Excellent cut flower; bumblebees and butterflies favorite. Top-down bloom.
THE monarch magnet — researchers consider it the top monarch nectar flower. Open buttons (vs spike) of L. spicata. Wet to medium soil.
Native tall (5-8 ft) wetland aster relative; deep purple fluffy fall flowers. Late-season butterfly nectar. Requires moist soil.
Native moist-meadow perennial; tall (5-7 ft) dome-shaped pink flower clusters. Top butterfly nectar plant. 'Gateway' is a compact cultivar.
Native blue-flowered hardy ageratum (often confused with annual ageratum). Late-season nectar. Spreads by rhizome - contain or use in informal areas.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 60 days

entries.notes: Western counterpart to common milkweed; large pink star-shaped flower clusters. Monarch host. Less aggressive than A. syriaca.

windows.notes: Cold stratify 60 days

entries.notes: Slender native milkweed (1-3 ft); whorled needle-like leaves. White flowers. Monarch host. CAUTION: toxic to grazing livestock.
Native wet-meadow perennial; white turtle-head shaped flowers. Sole host plant of Baltimore Checkerspot butterfly. Wet to medium soil.
Native eastern US; pink-rose turtle-head flowers, late summer. More drought-tolerant than white turtlehead. Bumblebee favorite.
Native moist-meadow perennial; brilliant scarlet flower spikes. Hummingbird magnet. Short-lived (3-5 years) but self-seeds readily.
Native moist-soil perennial; deep blue flower spikes. Bumblebee specialist. Hybridizes with cardinal flower (purple offspring).
Native legume; deep blue lupine-like spikes. Long-lived (50+ years), drought-tolerant. Don't transplant established clumps. Year 3 best bloom.
Native southeastern US; white pea-like flower spikes on tall stalks. Same long lifespan and drought tolerance as B. australis.
Southeastern native; yellow lupine-like spikes. More heat-tolerant than true lupines (Lupinus). Long-lived, deer-resistant.
Native eastern US shrub; white flower clusters. Nitrogen-fixer; deep roots. Leaves used as tea substitute during American Revolution.
California native shrub; clouds of fragrant blue flowers in spring. Pollinator powerhouse. Drought-tolerant once established. Skylark, Concha cultivars.
California native annual; pink/coral cup-shaped flowers. Self-seeds. Cool-season; bolts in heat (hence the name).
California native; ruffled pink/lavender/white flowers in tall spires. Self-seeds. Cool-spring annual.
California desert annual; deep blue bell-flowers. Distinct from the lacy phacelia (P. tanacetifolia) cover-crop entries; this one is purely ornamental.
Native plains perennial; red-and-yellow daisies, drought-tolerant. Parent of garden Gaillardia x grandiflora hybrids.
Garden hybrid; large multi-color daisies, blooms first year from seed. Arizona Sun, Goblin, Mesa series. Short-lived (3 years) but self-seeds.
Native prairie perennial; drooping yellow petals around dark coneflower head. 4-5 ft. Long bloom (June-September).
Native shortgrass-prairie perennial; tall thimble-shaped centers with drooping yellow/red petals. Drought-tolerant.
Native tallgrass-prairie perennial; perfoliate leaves form 'cups' that hold rainwater (bird drinking station). 6-10 ft, sunflower-like blooms.
Native tallgrass-prairie perennial; deep tap root, basal leaves orient north-south (the compass habit). 7-10 ft yellow flower spires year 3+.
Native umbel; gold flat-topped flower clusters in late spring. Larval host for Black Swallowtail butterfly. Beneficial-insect favorite.
Native prairie umbel; spiny yucca-like leaves, white globe flowers. Drought-tolerant; Native Americans used as snakebite treatment.
European herbaceous perennial; metallic blue flower clusters with thistly bracts. Salt-tolerant. Excellent dried flower.
Asian Agastache; lavender-blue spikes, anise-mint aroma. Both ornamental pollinator and culinary use (Korean perilla-mint banchan).
New Mexico native; pink flower spikes, long bloom. Hummingbird magnet. Drought- and heat-tolerant; deer-resistant.
Perennial Plant of the Year 1997; deep violet-blue flower spikes. Cut back after first flush for late-summer rebloom. Drought-tolerant.
Tender perennial Salvia hybrid; deep purple-black flowers. Massive pollinator magnet. Annual outside zone 7; treat as houseplant indoors in winter.
European perennial sage; blue-purple flower spikes. Caradonna, Marcus, Rose Marvel are top cultivars. Excellent for cottage and meadow gardens.
Native annual Monarda (texas/oklahoma); whorled lavender-pink flowers, lemon scent. Self-seeds. More heat-tolerant than M. didyma.
Perennial Plant of the Year 1998; horizontal-petal cultivar (vs reflexed in standard E. purpurea). Long-blooming, butterfly favorite.
White-petal cultivar with orange-bronze cone. Comes true from seed (rare for Echinacea cultivars). Bumblebee, butterfly, finch favorite.
AAS winner; compact (24 in) deep-pink coneflower that blooms first year from seed. Top choice for fast meadow establishment.
AAS Heritage; tall (4-5 ft) cosmos with 4 in white/pink/crimson flowers. Self-seeds; cut for vase use stimulates bloom.
Mexican native annual cosmos; orange-yellow-red daisies. Bright Lights, Cosmic series. More heat-tolerant than C. bipinnatus.

windows.notes: Plant tubers; lift in zones below 7

entries.notes: Mexican tuberous perennial cosmos; deep chocolate-maroon flowers smelling literally of cocoa. Hardy zone 7+; lift like dahlia in colder zones.
Mexican annual; the standard zinnia. California Giant, Lilliput, Cut & Come Again seed strains. Powdery mildew if crowded.
Compact disease-resistant series (mildew/leaf spot tolerant). 12-18 in mounding habit. AAS winner; reliable in humid southeastern gardens.
Tall (3-4 ft) cultivar with 5-6 in flowers. Strong stems for cutting. Mix or single colors available.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Zinnias

entries.notes: The flower-farmer standard; 4-6 in fully double blooms in 13 colors. Long stems (40 in), excellent vase life. Most bouquet-grade zinnia.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Zinnias

entries.notes: Classic 8-12 ft single-head sunflower; large edible seeds. Russian Mammoth, Mammoth Grey Stripe. The kid-garden gateway flower.
Branching multi-headed sunflower; mahogany-bronze-yellow color blend. 5-7 ft. Pollen-free strains exist for cut flower use.
Dwarf (2-3 ft) double-flowered sunflower; fluffy yellow pompoms. Good for containers and small gardens.
Branching 5-6 ft sunflower; deep maroon-velvet flowers. Cut-flower favorite. Continuous bloom from mid-summer through frost.
Heirloom branching sunflower; small (4 in) cream-white flowers with chocolate centers. Multi-headed, 5-7 ft.
Tall (6-8 ft) branching annual; pale lemon-yellow petals around chocolate centers. Top pollinator-attractor in Great Sunflower Project research.
Tall (2-3 ft) double-flowered African marigold; mixed yellow/orange/gold colors. Deer-resistant; pest-suppressive root exudates (nematodes).
Compact (12-14 in) French marigold; double mixed colors. Long bloom; reliable companion for tomatoes/peppers.
Heirloom French marigold; bright orange-and-mahogany ruffled crested flowers. Compact 12 in; classic edging plant.
Cultivated form of Flanders poppy; pink/red/white tissue-paper petals. Self-seeds in cool gardens. Source seed from extension/seed-bank, not roadside (could be invasive form).
Subarctic perennial usually grown as biennial; orange/yellow/pink/white papery flowers. Cool-summer climates only.
Heirloom breadseed/culinary poppy; seeds for baking, dried pods for crafts. Latex contains opium alkaloids - cultivation legal but extraction is federally controlled.
Perennial poppy; large 4-6 in red/orange/pink flowers in late spring. Foliage dies back in summer (interplant with later perennials to fill gap).
Trailing crimson amaranth; 4-6 ft tall, dramatic dangling flower tassels. Self-seeds. Edible grain like other amaranths.
Cottage-garden biennial; large 2 in bell flowers in pink/blue/white. Sow year 1 for year 2 bloom. Cut spent stalks for self-seeding.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cottage Garden Annuals

entries.notes: Biennial dianthus; clusters of pink/red/white spice-clove-scented flowers. Edible petals (remove white heel). Self-seeds.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cottage Garden Biennials

entries.notes: Tall (2-3 ft) annual with apple-green calyces stacked along stem; tiny white flowers within. Excellent cut and dried flower.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Annuals

entries.notes: Australian everlasting; papery petals dry without losing color. Premier dried flower; cut at half-open stage.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Dried Flower Annuals

entries.notes: Mediterranean annual; papery winged flower clusters in purple/blue/white/pink. Salt-tolerant. Excellent cut and dried.
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Cut Flower Annuals

entries.notes: Brain-coral-shaped fasciated flower heads in red/yellow/pink/orange. Heat-loving. Excellent cut and dried.
Feathery plume-shaped flower heads; same colors as cockscomb. Forest Fire and Castle series are reliable bedding cultivars.
Wheat-shaped narrow flower spikes in pink/cream. Flamingo Feather and Pink Candle cultivars. Cut-flower farmer favorite.
Tropical perennial often grown as annual; flowers open mid-afternoon (4 PM), fragrant. Multi-color blooms on same plant. Tubers hardy zone 7+.

windows.notes: Nick seed coats for germination

entries.notes: Tropical climbing vine; large 5-6 in white fragrant flowers open at dusk. Hawkmoth-pollinated. Trellis 10+ ft.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24 hours

entries.notes: Heavenly Blue, Grandpa Ott's, Scarlett O'Hara. Climbing vines (8-10 ft); flowers open at dawn. Self-seeds; can naturalize aggressively.

windows.notes: Soak seed 24 hours

entries.notes: 1699 Sicilian heirloom — the original bicolor purple-and-maroon sweet pea. Strongest fragrance of any cultivar. Toxic seeds (do not eat).
entries.source: Cornell Cooperative Extension - Heirloom Sweet Peas

windows.notes: Plant bulbs after first frost

entries.notes: Tall (24-30 in) classic-shape tulip; the standard 'cut flower' tulip. Treat as annual in zones 8+ (no winter chill); perennializes 3-5 years in zones 3-7.
Wild species tulip ('water lily tulip'); 6-10 in dwarf, naturalizes well. Earlier bloom than Darwin types. Long-lived in zones 3-7.
Long-lived perennial; deer- and rodent-proof (toxic alkaloids). Naturalizes in lawns. Allow foliage to die back naturally for next year's bloom.
Earliest spring bulb (often blooms through snow). Naturalizes in lawns. Distinct from saffron-yielding C. sativus (autumn-blooming, separate entry).
Small (6-9 in) blue grape-cluster flowers; naturalizes aggressively. Foliage emerges in fall, persists through winter. Spreads by bulb offsets and seed.

windows.notes: Plant 'in the green' if possible (right after bloom) for best establishment

entries.notes: Earliest spring bulb in many zones; nodding white bell flowers through snow. Long-lived; spreads slowly. Toxic to humans (alkaloids).
Naturalizing woodland bulb; blue/pink/white nodding bells. More vigorous than English bluebell - can crowd out natives in shade gardens.
Shade groundcover; intensely fragrant white bell flowers in May. ALL PARTS HIGHLY TOXIC (cardiac glycosides). Spreads aggressively by rhizome.
